original: His big brown eyes are the same color as melted chocolate in a pan, glistening, rich. He blinks and then nods. She knew he wouldn’t be able to resist that. It is all he longs for, news about his bloody wayward stepmother. It infuriates her. Kylie was so undeserving. She whispers, “You have to stay absolutely silent, agreed?” He nods again. She smiles at him and takes her hand away from his mouth.
paraphrase: His large, dark-brown eyes, shimmering and deep, are the exact shade of liquefied chocolate warming in a pot. He blinks once, then inclines his head. She had anticipated this, knowing he wouldn't be able to turn down the offer. Information regarding his troublesome, disobedient stepmother is precisely what he craves above all else. This situation fills her with intense anger. Kylie, in her estimation, was entirely unworthy. She murmurs softly, "You must remain completely quiet, do we understand each other?" He responds with another nod. She offers him a small smile and removes her hand from his lips.

original: The Hawker was low in the sky now, skimming the treetops to their right. Simon Edwards went downstairs to watch the landing from tarmac level. The Kent police were poised, just out of sight, and the maintenance man waited with his wedges. Out on the runway, the Hawker's nose tipped up, and the tires touched down in a puff of smoke. The plane settled in for deceleration, streaking from right to left in front of the terminal, its white hull glistening in the wet weather.
paraphrase: The Hawker aircraft, now positioned low in the atmosphere, was passing closely over the treetops situated to their right. Simon Edwards proceeded to the lower level of the building to observe the descent from the ground of the airfield. The Kent police force was prepared, remaining unseen momentarily, while the maintenance worker stood ready with his chocks. On the runway, the front section of the Hawker elevated, and the wheels made contact with the surface, generating a cloud of smoke. The aircraft then began its slowing process, moving rapidly from the right side to the left in front of the main building, its white exterior gleaming under the damp weather conditions.

original: The Sultanas nonsense about lovers had clearly scrambled my senses. Arin was attractiveit was as obvious and indisputable as the sun. But I had spent nearly twenty-one years capable of acknowledging attractiveness without being attracted myself. I had never wanted anyone, never yearned for the physical relationships Marek chased. I finally empathized with the girls in the keep. Especially Gana. The fanciful ward used to dress in Rayas finest gowns every week, sing warbling ballads while dabbing fragrances on her wrists and behind her ear. There is power in conquering the unconquerable, Gana had said one year, after rejecting yet another fellows advances. The keep had gone to Zeilas for celebratory tea and ahwa after a successful market. Zeila laid reed rugs on the floor, and we sat on beaded cushions, a wooden table wobbling at our feet. Id been a few cushions down with Sefa and Marek, sipping my bitter ahwa from a chipped cup. Ganas conversation with Daleel had reached my ears. Men dont see women, dear Daleel. They see power. Which one of us has more of it, and how easily they can drain it out of her. Apparently, this wasnt a trait reserved for men, because a dark thrill raced through me at the thought of conquering the Nizahl Heir. Stealing a piece of Arins power in the surrender. The breeze ruffled Arins hair. He chuckled, drawing me back to the present. No attendants are permitted in my quarters.
paraphrase: The foolish talk from Sultanas regarding romantic partners had undoubtedly muddled my perception. Arin possessed an undeniable attractiveness, a fact as self-evident and undeniable as the sun itself. However, for almost twenty-one years, I had been perfectly capable of recognizing beauty without experiencing personal attraction. I had never desired anyone, nor had I ever longed for the kind of physical intimacy that Marek actively pursued. I finally understood the feelings of the women residing in the keep, particularly Gana. The imaginative ward would routinely adorn herself in Raya's most exquisite dresses each week, crooning melodic songs while applying perfumes to her wrists and behind her ear. "There is immense satisfaction in overcoming that which seems insurmountable," Gana had declared one year, following her rejection of yet another suitor's advances. The keep's inhabitants had traveled to Zeila's dwelling for a celebratory tea and ahwa after a prosperous market day. Zeila had spread reed mats across the floor, and we reclined on plush beaded cushions, a somewhat unsteady wooden table positioned at our feet. I had been seated a few cushions away with Sefa and Marek, slowly drinking my robust ahwa from a cup that was slightly chipped. Gana's discussion with Daleel had incidentally reached my hearing. "Men do not truly perceive women, my dear Daleel. They perceive influence. They assess which of us possesses more of it, and how effortlessly they can extract it from her." Evidently, this characteristic was not exclusively confined to men, as a profound excitement surged through me at the notion of subduing the Nizahl Heir. I envisioned seizing a portion of Arin's authority in the act of his capitulation. The gentle wind stirred Arin's hair. He let out a soft laugh, which brought my attention back to the current moment. "No personal assistants are allowed within my chambers."

The hot weather has returned and the carriage is stifling today, despite being only half full. I was late getting up this morning and didn’t have time to pick up a newspaper or to check the news on the Internet before I left the house, so  I am trying to get the BBC site on my phone, but for some reason it is taking forever to load. original: ‘That’s the daughter of the man who owns Wormwood Motors in the village,’ Miss Trunchbull barked. She hardly ever spoke in a normal voice. She either barked or shouted. ‘An excellent person, Wormwood,’ she went on. ‘I was in there only yesterday. He sold me a car. Almost new. Only done ten thousand miles. Previous owner was an old lady who took it out once a year at the most. A terrific bargain. Yes, I liked Wormwood. A real pillar of our society. He told me the daughter was a bad lot though. He said to watch her. He said if anything bad ever happened in the school, it was certain to be his daughter who did it. I haven’t met the little brat yet, but she’ll know about it when I do. Her father said she’s a real wart.’
paraphrase: "The individual you're referring to is the offspring of the proprietor of Wormwood Motors, located in our village," Miss Trunchbull declared in a harsh tone. She very rarely communicated in a standard speaking manner, instead typically resorting to either bellowing or shouting. She continued, "Wormwood is a remarkably fine individual. Just yesterday, I paid a visit to his establishment. He provided me with an automobile that was practically new, having only accrued ten thousand miles. Its previous owner was an elderly woman who drove it a maximum of once annually. A truly incredible deal, it was. Indeed, I hold Wormwood in high regard; he is a genuine cornerstone of our community. However, he did inform me that his daughter was a troublesome character. He cautioned me to keep a close eye on her. He stated that should any unfortunate incidents occur within the school, his daughter would undoubtedly be the perpetrator. I have not yet encountered the young hooligan, but she will certainly become acquainted with my presence when I do. Her father characterized her as a veritable nuisance."

original: The door closed, leaving me in the shadows. I stayed there a few moments and then dragged myself down the stairs. The rain was still pouring down, relentlessly. I walked off down the street. When I reached the corner, I stopped and turned around for a moment. I looked up at the Aguilars' apartment. I could see the silhouette of my old friend Tomas outlined against his bedroom window. He was staring at me, motionless. I waved at him but he didn't return the greeting. A few seconds later, he moved away to the back of the room. I waited almost five minutes, hoping he would reappear, but he didn't.
paraphrase: As the door shut, I was enveloped in darkness. I lingered there briefly before slowly making my way down the staircase. Outside, the rain continued to fall without ceasing. I proceeded along the street. Upon reaching the intersection, I paused and glanced back for a moment. My gaze drifted upwards towards the Aguilars' residence. There, silhouetted against his bedroom window, was the recognizable outline of my long-time companion, Tomas. He was observing me, completely still. I raised my hand in a wave, but he did not reciprocate the gesture. A short while later, he retreated further into the room. I remained there for nearly five minutes, holding onto the hope that he would emerge again, but he did not.

original: She went to the garbage can, tipped open the lid, and stopped. Pupkin was gone. Hey! Mark said behind her. Louise jumped. She turned and saw him staring at her through the open garage door. pupkin isnt there, hes escaped, hell be so angry She dropped the Squirrel Nativity into the can and slammed the lid shut. Hey is for horses, she said, a favorite saying of their moms. I know what youre doing. He smiled. You think Im going to freak out over how long this is taking and cut you in on the sale. She locked her face into its blankest expression and asked in an innocent voice, Youre planning on selling? Homeownership is for suckers, he said.
paraphrase: She approached the refuse bin, flipped open its cover, and then paused. Pupkin had vanished. "Hey!" Mark exclaimed from behind her. Louise flinched. She spun around and observed him gazing at her through the unlatched garage entrance. "Pupkin isn't here, he's gotten away, he's going to be so furious." She released the Squirrel Nativity into the container and forcefully shut the lid. "Hey is for horses," she stated, a beloved idiom of their mother's. "I'm aware of your intentions." He grinned. "You believe I'm going to panic about the time this is consuming and offer you a share of the profits." She adopted her most expressionless facade and inquired with an unassuming tone, "Are you intending to sell?" "Property ownership is for fools," he declared.

original: This is where the bed I shared with my sister, Prim, stood. Over there was the kitchen table. The bricks of the chimney, which collapsed in a charred heap, provide a point of reference for the rest of the house. How else could I orient myself in this sea of gray? Almost nothing remains of District 12. A month ago, the Capitol’s firebombs obliterated the poor coal miners’ houses in the Seam, the shops in the town, even the Justice Building. The only area that escaped incineration was the Victor’s Village. I don’t know why exactly. Perhaps so anyone forced to come here on Capitol business would have somewhere decent to stay. The odd reporter. A committee assessing the condition of the coal mines. A squad of Peacekeepers checking for returning refugees. But no one is returning except me. And that’s only for a brief visit. The authorities in District 13 were against my coming back. They viewed it as a costly and pointless venture, given that at least a dozen invisible hovercraft are circling overhead for my protection and there’s no intelligence to be gained. I had to see it, though. So much so that I made it a condition of my cooperating with any of their plans. Finally, Plutarch Heavensbee, the Head Gamemaker who had organized the rebels in the Capitol, threw up his hands. Better to waste a day than another month. Maybe a little tour of Twelve is just what she needs to convince her we’re on the same side.” The same side.
paraphrase: Here is where my sister, Prim, and I shared a bed. The kitchen table was located on the opposite side of the room. The charred remains of the chimney, which had crumbled into a pile, serve as a landmark for the other parts of the house. In this expanse of gray, how else would I be able to orient myself? Barely anything is left of District 12. A month prior, the Capitol's firebombs completely destroyed the humble homes of the coal miners in the Seam, the various establishments in the town, and even the Justice Building. The sole section that avoided being burned down was the Victor’s Village. I'm not entirely sure why. Perhaps it was so that anyone compelled to visit for Capitol affairs would have a suitable place to stay. An occasional journalist. A committee evaluating the state of the coal mines. A group of Peacekeepers searching for returning refugees. However, no one is coming back except for me. And my visit is merely temporary. The authorities in District 13 opposed my return. They considered it an expensive and futile undertaking, especially since at least twelve invisible hovercraft are circling above me for security, and there's no useful information to be acquired. Nevertheless, I felt I had to witness it. So much so that I made it a prerequisite for my collaboration with any of their schemes. Ultimately, Plutarch Heavensbee, the Head Gamemaker responsible for organizing the rebels in the Capitol, conceded. "It's better to lose a day than another month. Perhaps a short trip to Twelve is precisely what she needs to persuade her that we are allies." Allies.

original: Then take note of who helps you eagerly and who reluctantly. Make sure that helpful people are favored—give them the jobs they like to do, and make sure the unhelpful ones get all the dirty work. Then people will start to realize that it pays to oblige the countess. They will also love you much more than William, who isn’t very lovable anyway. Eventually  you will become a power in your own right. Most countesses are.”   “You make it sound easy,” Elizabeth said wistfully. Every now and again the wind would howl and wake Aliena. Looking around in the fitful candlelight she saw that most of the adults were doing the same, sitting upright, nodding off for a while, then waking up suddenly. Almost everyone around her was fast asleep. She shifted her position, lying flat on the floor, and wrapped her cloak tightly around her. The storm was not letting up, but people’s need for sleep had overcome their anxiety. The sound of the rain blowing against the walls of the church was like waves crashing on a beach, and instead of keeping her awake it now lulled her to sleep. She wondered what had disturbed her. She listened: silence. The storm had ended. A faint gray light seeped in through the windows. All the villagers were fast asleep. Her movement disturbed Elizabeth, who came awake instantly. They went to the church door, opened it, and stepped outside. The sun had not yet risen, but the dawn sky was pearl-gray.
paraphrase:
Next, observe who readily offers assistance and who does so unwillingly. Ensure that those who are helpful are rewarded—assign them desirable tasks and guarantee that the unhelpful individuals are given all the undesirable chores. Consequently, people will begin to understand the advantages of accommodating the countess. Furthermore, they will hold you in much higher regard than William, who is not particularly endearing in any case. In due course, you will establish yourself as a formidable figure. Most countesses achieve this status.”

“You make it seem straightforward,” Elizabeth remarked with a hint of longing. Periodically, the wind would shriek, stirring Aliena from her slumber. Glancing around in the flickering candlelight, she observed that most of the grown-ups were similarly affected, sitting upright, dozing intermittently, then abruptly awakening. Nearly everyone else surrounding her was deeply asleep. She adjusted her posture, stretching out flat on the ground, and drew her cloak snugly around herself. The tempest showed no signs of abating, yet the human need for rest had conquered their apprehension. The sound of the rain pelting against the church walls was reminiscent of ocean waves crashing ashore, and rather than preventing her sleep, it now gently coaxed her into slumber. She pondered what had roused her. She listened: complete quiet. The storm had passed. A soft, gray illumination filtered in through the apertures. All the inhabitants of the village were profoundly asleep. Her movement roused Elizabeth, who immediately became alert. They proceeded to the church entrance, unlatched it, and stepped out into the open. The sun had not yet emerged, but the pre-dawn sky was a delicate pearl-gray hue.

original: Bert Baxter was reciting a dirty poem about an Eskimo and Mrs Harriman had a funny turn and had to have her corsets loosened. I said, ‘Bert Baxter is not so bad when you get to know him’. She said she didn’t understand why the Good Lord took my grandad and left scum like Baxter. Then she pulled her lips tight and dabbled her eyes with a handkerchief, so we left. He must be cracking up!   Friday March 6 th   New Moon   Mr Cherry is very pleased with my work and he has raised my wages by two and a halfpence an hour. He also offered me the Corporation Row evening round, but I declined his offer. Corporation Row is where thecouncil put all the bad tenants. Barry Kent lives at number 13. He told me not tell my mother. As if I would! I have put them under my mattress. Intellectuals like me are allowed to be interested in sex. It is ordinary people like Mr Lucas who should be ashamed of themselves. I told a lie and said I was his grandson. They are sending a social worker to see him on Monday. I have lost my own. They got on well. Felt  like I have never  felt  before. The dog had twisted itscollar on the gear lever and its eyes were bulging out of its head. When it saw me it tried to jump up and nearly killed itself. The car was locked so I was forced to break the little window open and unlock the door by putting my hand through.
paraphrase:
Bert Baxter was in the middle of reciting a rather inappropriate poem focusing on an Eskimo when Mrs. Harriman suddenly experienced a dizzy spell, necessitating the loosening of her corsets. I remarked, "Bert Baxter isn't really that bad once you get to know him." Her response was that she couldn't comprehend why the Almighty had chosen to take my grandfather but allowed scoundrels like Baxter to remain. Following this, she pressed her lips together firmly and dabbed her eyes with a handkerchief, which prompted our departure. He must be losing his mind!

Friday, March 6th, marked the New Moon. Mr. Cherry has expressed great satisfaction with my performance and has increased my hourly wage by two and a halfpence. He also presented me with the opportunity to take on the Corporation Row evening delivery route, but I chose to decline his proposal. Corporation Row is the area where the council houses all the problematic tenants. Barry Kent resides at number 13. He cautioned me against informing my mother. As if I ever would! I have hidden them beneath my mattress. Individuals of intellectual inclination, such as myself, are permitted to have an interest in sex. It is common folk, like Mr. Lucas, who ought to feel a sense of shame. I fabricated a story, claiming to be his grandson. A social worker is scheduled to visit him on Monday. I have misplaced my own belongings. They had a good rapport. I felt a sensation I had never experienced previously. The dog had managed to twist its collar around the gear lever, and its eyes were protruding from its head. Upon seeing me, it attempted to leap up and very nearly harmed itself fatally. The car was locked, compelling me to smash open the small window and reach my hand through to unlock the door.

original: If you could have a chat with God about some questions and doubts Im having, then come and explain them to me, Id appreciate that. Dont feel like you cant come and visit me, not in a spooky wayI scare easybut you know, in like a dream or a voice in my head? Although, not in a way that will make me think Im having a psychotic breakdown. Ill leave the technicalities up to you. Have a chat with the other souls up there and see what the preferred method of communication between the living and the dead is. Anyway, Dad I take a deep breath. Im sorry we had to say goodbye to you today. Im sorry I wanted to leave the house, to move out and find out who I was. Especially since it was all for nothing; Im not entirely convinced Im any better than I was before. Maybe, thats a bit harsh. I think of Alex. I learned some things. I think of Ben. I learned a lot of things. I think of Mum and James. Actually, Im going to take that back; it wasnt all for nothing. But Im sorry that on some days the guilt melts away and I feel relieved about not having to worry about you so much. Id have you back in a heartbeat if  I probably shouldnt have said any of that. Please dont hate me. I pause, thinking about what Angelina said about love and all its varied forms of expression.
paraphrase:
I would be grateful if you could engage in a conversation with the Almighty regarding some inquiries and uncertainties I possess, and subsequently elucidate them to me. Please do not hesitate to reach out to me, not in a supernatural or frightening manner – as I am easily startled – but perhaps through a dream or an inner voice. However, I would prefer a method that does not lead me to believe I am experiencing a psychotic episode. I will defer the specifics of this communication to your discretion. Kindly consult with the other departed spirits and ascertain the most favorable mode of interaction between those who are living and those who have passed on.

Furthermore, Dad, I inhale deeply. I regret that we were compelled to bid you farewell today. I apologize for my desire to depart from our home, to relocate and embark on a journey of self-discovery. This sentiment is particularly poignant given that it ultimately proved to be futile; I am not entirely convinced that I have improved beyond my previous state. Perhaps that assessment is somewhat severe. I recall Alex. I did acquire some knowledge. I recall Ben. I gained a significant amount of insight. I recall Mum and James. In fact, I retract my previous statement; it was not entirely without purpose. Nevertheless, I am sorry that on certain days, the feeling of guilt dissipates, and I experience a sense of relief regarding the diminished need to be concerned about you. I would welcome your return instantly if... I likely should not have vocalized any of that. Please, do not harbor any animosity towards me. I momentarily cease, contemplating Angelina's observations concerning love and its diverse manifestations.

original: Is there any chance of a third, do you think? Perhaps. But itll come with a fee. Ill pay whatever you want. He stopped typing to look at her. Tell me your price. Iris bit her lip, wondering what she should ask for. Are you sure about that, Kitt? What if I want you to wash my laundry for the rest of the war? What if I want you to massage my feet every night? What if I want you to make me a cup of tea every hour? I can do all of that and more if you like, he said, deadly serious. Simply tell me what you want. She breathed, slow and deep, trying to dim the fire that seemed so eager to burn within her. That blue-hearted fire that Roman sparked. He was watching, waiting, and she dropped her eyes to where she had left her sentence hanging on the page. The explosion. His hand being ripped from hers. The smoke that rose. Why had she been unscathed, when so many others hadnt? Men and women who had given so much more than her, who would never get to return home to their families, their lovers. Who would never see their next birthday, or kiss the person they least expected, or grow old and wise, watching flowers bloom in their garden. I dont deserve this, she whispered. She felt like she was betraying her brother. Lieutenant Lark. The Sycamore Platoon.
paraphrase: Do you believe there's a possibility of a third? Perhaps, but it will incur a cost. I am prepared to pay any amount you request. He paused his typing to direct his gaze at her. State your price. Iris nibbled her lip, contemplating her demands. Are you absolutely certain, Kitt? What if I requested you to handle my laundry for the duration of the conflict? What if I desired a foot massage from you every evening? What if I wished for you to prepare a cup of tea for me every sixty minutes? He responded with grave seriousness, "I am capable of all of that and more, should you desire it. Simply articulate your wishes." She inhaled slowly and deeply, attempting to subdue the intense fervor that seemed determined to ignite within her. That azure-hearted inferno ignited by Roman. He observed, patiently awaiting her response, and she lowered her gaze to the point where she had left her sentence unfinished on the page. The detonation. His hand forcibly separated from hers. The ascending smoke. Why had she emerged unharmed, while countless others had not? Individuals, both male and female, who had contributed far more than she, who would never again return to their loved ones, their partners. Who would never witness another birthday, or share an unexpected kiss, or attain old age and wisdom, observing blossoms unfurl in their gardens. "I am unworthy of this," she murmured. She felt as though she were betraying her sibling. Lieutenant Lark. The Sycamore Platoon.

original: In one corner stood a marble woman, to whom her own beauty was the sole and sufficient garment. Some pictures--that looked old, and had a mellow tinge diffused through all their artful splendor--hung on the walls. Near the fireplace was a large and very beautiful cabinet of ebony, inlaid with ivory; a piece of antique furniture, which Mr. Pyncheon had bought in Venice, and which he used as the treasure-place for medals, ancient coins, and whatever small and valuable curiosities he had picked up on his travels.

Close to the hearth, there stood a substantial and exquisitely crafted cabinet made of ebony, adorned with intricate ivory inlays; this was an old piece of furniture that Mr. Pyncheon had acquired in Venice, and he utilized it as a repository for medals, old coins, and any other small yet valuable collectible items he had gathered during his journeys.

original: His mind was haunted with the many and strange tales which he had heard, attributing mysterious if not supernatural endowments to these Maules, as well the grandson here present as his two immediate ancestors. Mr. Pyncheon's long residence abroad, and intercourse with men of wit and fashion, --courtiers, worldings, and free-thinkers,--had done much towards obliterating the grim Puritan superstitions, which no man of New England birth at that early period could entirely escape. But, on the other hand, had not a whole Community believed Maule's grandfather to be a wizard? Had not the crime been proved? Had not the wizard died for it? Had he not bequeathed a legacy of hatred against the Pyncheons to this only grandson, who, as it appeared, was now about to exercise a subtle influence over the daughter of his enemy's house? Might not this influence be the same that was called witchcraft? Turning half around, he caught a glimpse of Maule's figure in the looking-glass. At some paces from Alice, with his arms uplifted in the air, the carpenter made a gesture as if directing downward a slow, ponderous, and invisible weight upon the maiden. "Stay, Maule!" exclaimed Mr. Pyncheon, stepping forward. "I forbid your proceeding further!" "Pray, my dear father, do not interrupt the young man," said Alice, without changing her position. "His efforts, I assure you, will prove very harmless." Again Mr. Pyncheon turned his eyes towards the Claude. It was then his daughter's will, in opposition to his own, that the experiment should be fully tried.
paraphrase: The numerous and peculiar accounts he had encountered, which ascribed enigmatic, if not otherworldly, capabilities to the Maule family—including the grandson currently present and his two direct forefathers—occupied his thoughts persistently. Mr. Pyncheon's extended stay in foreign lands, coupled with his interactions with individuals of intellect and refinement—such as aristocrats, socialites, and liberal thinkers—had significantly contributed to the eradication of the stern Puritan beliefs, which no individual born in New England during that nascent era could wholly elude. Nevertheless, had not an entire populace held the conviction that Maule's grandfather was a sorcerer? Had the transgression not been substantiated? Had the sorcerer not perished as a consequence? Had he not bequeathed a heritage of animosity toward the Pyncheons to this sole descendant, who, by all indications, was now poised to exert an elusive sway over the offspring of his adversary's lineage? Could this sway potentially be synonymous with what was termed sorcery? Pivoting partially, he discerned Maule's form reflected in the mirror. Several paces distant from Alice, with his arms elevated, the craftsman executed a motion as if guiding a deliberate, weighty, and imperceptible burden downwards upon the young woman. "Halt, Maule!" Mr. Pyncheon interjected, advancing. "I prohibit any further action from you!" "Please, my esteemed father, do not impede the young gentleman," Alice stated, maintaining her posture. "His endeavors, I guarantee, will prove entirely innocuous." Once more, Mr. Pyncheon directed his gaze towards the Claude. It was, at that moment, his daughter's desire, contradicting his own, that the undertaking should be thoroughly conducted.

For a moment everyone thought he was going to be successful, for the man breathed hard and muttered, “I’ll go eastward no more. Out oars for Narnia.” But he sank back almost at once into a yet deeper sleep than before: that is, his heavy head sagged a few inches lower toward the table and all efforts to rouse him again were useless.

However, he almost immediately relapsed into an even profounder state of sleep than previously; specifically, his heavy head drooped several inches closer to the table, rendering all subsequent attempts to awaken him fruitless.

original: ‘One step beyond that boundary line which resembles the line dividing the living from the dead lies uncertainty, suffering, and death. And what is there? Who is there?there beyond that field, that tree, that roof lit up by the sun? No one knows, but one wants to know. You fear and yet long to cross that line, and know that sooner or later it must be crossed and you will have to find out what is there, just as you will inevitably have to learn what lies the other side of death.

Who inhabits that space, situated beyond that expanse of land, that arboreal feature, that sun-drenched architectural covering? The answer eludes everyone, yet there's an inherent desire to ascertain it. You experience apprehension while simultaneously yearning to traverse that threshold, recognizing that its crossing is an eventual certainty, and you will ultimately be compelled to discover its contents, much like you are destined to uncover what awaits beyond the precipice of mortality.

original: Valancourt talked long by the side of the carriage; seemed more than once to be going, but still lingered, and appeared to search anxiously for topics of conversation to account for his delay. At length he took leave. As he went, St. Aubert observed him look with an earnest and pensive eye at Emily, who bowed to him with a countenance full of timid sweetness, while the carriage drove on. St. Aubert, for whatever reason, soon after looked from the window, and saw Valancourt standing upon the bank of the road, resting on his pike with folded arms, and following the carriage with his eyes. He waved his hand, and Valancourt, seeming to awake from his reverie, returned the salute, and started away. The aspect of the country now began to change, and the travellers soon found themselves among mountains covered from their base nearly to their summits with forests of gloomy pine, except where a rock of granite shot up from the vale, and lost its snowy top in the clouds. The rivulet, which had hitherto accompanied them, now expanded into a river; and, flowing deeply and silently along, reflected, as in a mirror, the blackness of the impending shades. Sometimes a cliff was seen lifting its bold head above the woods and the vapours, that floated mid-way down the mountains; and sometimes a face of perpendicular marble rose from the water's edge, over which the larch threw his gigantic arms, here scathed with lightning, and there floating in luxuriant foliage.
paraphrase: Valancourt engaged in an extended conversation beside the carriage; on several occasions, he appeared to be departing, yet he continued to tarry, seemingly striving intently to find subjects for discussion to justify his prolonged presence. Eventually, he bid farewell. As he departed, St. Aubert noticed him gaze with an intense and thoughtful expression at Emily, who acknowledged him with a face radiating modest charm, as the carriage proceeded. St. Aubert, for reasons unknown, shortly thereafter peered out of the window and observed Valancourt standing on the roadside bank, leaning on his pike with his arms crossed, and tracking the carriage with his eyes. He extended his hand in a wave, and Valancourt, seemingly roused from his deep contemplation, reciprocated the greeting and then abruptly moved on. The landscape now began to undergo a transformation, and the travelers quickly found themselves amidst mountainous terrain, blanketed from their foundations almost to their peaks with dense forests of somber pine, except in areas where a granite rock protruded from the valley, its snow-capped summit disappearing into the clouds. The small stream that had, until this point, journeyed alongside them, now broadened into a substantial river; and, as it flowed deeply and quietly, it mirrored, as if in a looking glass, the profound darkness of the overhanging shadows. Occasionally, a precipice was visible, thrusting its prominent crown above the woodlands and the mists that drifted halfway down the mountain slopes; and sometimes, a sheer face of marble ascended directly from the water's brink, over which the larch tree extended its immense branches, some scarred by lightning, and others adorned with abundant greenery.

original: But I neither longed for nor resented him, as Id always sensed he believed. Though Id been hurt and humiliated by his rejection, it had, I soon realized, freed me and offered clarity. I would never again risk poisoning TNO for myself by falling for or trying to date anyone there. And this decision made me see that there was a different way I wrote when, even subconsciously, I was seeking male approval, male sexual approval: a more coy way, more reserved, more nervous about being perceived as angry or vulgar. It was the syntactical equivalent of dressing up as a sexy zombie for Halloween.
paraphrase: I neither yearned for him nor felt any bitterness towards him, contrary to what I had always perceived he thought. While his rejection had undeniably caused me pain and embarrassment, I quickly understood that it had, in fact, liberated me and brought about a new level of understanding. I resolved never again to jeopardize my experience at TNO by developing romantic feelings for or attempting to date anyone employed there. This particular resolution illuminated a distinct manner in which I composed my writing, specifically when, even without conscious awareness, I was pursuing the affirmation of men, particularly their sexual approval. My writing became more demure, more constrained, and exhibited a greater apprehension of being labeled as enraged or crude. It was, in essence, the grammatical counterpart to adorning oneself in a provocative zombie costume for the Halloween festivities.

When she lowered herself into the chair, there was a loud squelching noise similar to that made by a hippopotamus when lowering its foot into the mud on the banks of the Limpopo River.

original: Boys stood up and shouted and Ralph shouted back. "Because if you want a fire to cook fish or crab, you can jolly well go up the mountain. That way we'll be certain." Hands were reaching for the conch in the light of the setting sun. He held on and leapt on the trunk. "All this I meant to say. Now I've said it. You voted me for chief. Now you do what I say." They quieted, slowly, and at last were seated again. Ralph dropped down and spoke in his ordinary voice. "So remember. The rocks for a lavatory. Keep the fire going and smoke showing as a signal.
paraphrase: The boys rose to their feet, yelling, and Ralph responded in kind. "Should you desire a fire for preparing fish or crab, you are entirely welcome to ascend the mountain. This approach guarantees our success." In the glow of the declining sun, hands extended towards the conch. He gripped it firmly and sprang onto the tree trunk. "These are all the points I intended to make. I have now conveyed them. You selected me as your leader. Therefore, you will adhere to my directives." They gradually fell silent, eventually resuming their seats. Ralph descended and addressed them in his typical tone. "So, bear in mind these instructions. The designated area for the lavatory is the rocks. Maintain the fire and ensure smoke is visible as a signal.

original: There was nothing to know. Nothing that could be proved. The trees opened up at the edge of the pond. Frogs creaked and croaked in the water, and insects danced along the surface. The scene had a bedraggled kind of charm to it, but the magic of that summer was long gone. Is it at all possible that you were mistaken? Ethan asked, his voice keeping me brutishly anchored in the present. I wished he would leaveand I was glad he didnt. Time here was slippery. I didnt want to be alone with the past. Can you see any possibility that the man who attacked you wasnt Stahl, but someone else? Will you just let it go? Im not going to play along with your pet theory, I said. Naomi. He grabbed my arm. I snarled, wrenching away from him, and turned with my fingers tightening into fists. But he wasnt looking at me. His gaze shot past me. I started to turn. He reached for me again, as if to stop me, but he couldnt. Nothing could stop this, the moment when endless possibility collapsed into the cruel certainty of fact. It was her hand I saw first, fingers bent. And then the oil slick of her hair seeping out across the surface of the water, obscuring all but a fractured sliver of her cheek. Then the dark blotch of her torso, loose shirt billowing around her, obscuring the shape of her until my mind refused to see that it was a person at all, was more than this collection of fragments.
paraphrase: There was an absence of verifiable facts. No information that could be conclusively demonstrated. At the pond's edge, the trees parted. Within the water, frogs emitted their characteristic creaks and croaks, while insects skittered across its surface. The tableau possessed a somewhat disheveled appeal, yet the enchantment of that particular summer had long since vanished. "Is there even a remote chance you might be mistaken?" inquired Ethan, his tone forcibly grounding me in the immediate moment. I longed for his departure, yet simultaneously felt relief that he remained. Time in this place felt elusive. I had no desire to confront the past by myself. "Is it conceivable that the individual who assaulted you wasn't Stahl, but rather another person entirely?" "Will you simply drop the subject?" I retorted. "I have no intention of indulging your speculative hypothesis." "Naomi." He seized my arm. I let out a low growl, pulling free from his grasp, and spun around, my fingers clenching into tight fists. However, his attention was not on me. His gaze darted beyond me. I began to pivot. He reached for me once more, seemingly to restrain me, but his attempt was futile. Nothing could impede this unfolding event, this instant where boundless potential dissolved into the harsh finality of reality. Her hand was the initial detail I registered, her fingers bent at an unnatural angle. Following that, the dark sheen of her hair spread across the water's surface, concealing everything except a fragmented glimpse of her cheek. original: Some called for ladders, some for sledge-hammers; some ran with torches to and fro as if to seek them, and still came back and roared again; some spent their breath in impotent curses and execrations; some pressed forward with the ecstasy of madmen, and thus impeded the progress of those below; some among the boldest attempted to climb up by the water-spout and crevices in the wall; and all waved to and fro, in the darkness beneath, like a field of corn moved by an angry wind: and joined from time to time in one loud furious roar. 'The tide,' cried the murderer, as he staggered back into the room, and shut the faces out, 'the tide was in as I came up.
paraphrase: A variety of demands were made for equipment, with some requesting ladders and others calling for sledge-hammers; certain individuals dashed about with torches, seemingly in search of something, only to return repeatedly and shout once more; others exhausted themselves with futile curses and condemnations; some surged ahead with an almost insane fervor, thereby obstructing the movement of those behind them; among the most daring, a few endeavored to ascend by means of the water-spout and the cracks in the wall; and the entire gathering swayed back and forth in the gloom beneath, resembling a field of grain agitated by a fierce gale: occasionally uniting in a single, powerful, and enraged bellow. "The water level," exclaimed the killer, as he stumbled back into the chamber, blocking out the sight of the faces, "the tide had come in as I approached."

"Dorian, if I told you, you might like me less than you do, and you would certainly laugh at me. I could not bear your doing either of those two things. If you wish me never to look at your picture again, I am content. I have always you to look at. If you wish the best work I have ever done to be hidden from the world, I am satisfied. Your friendship is dearer to me than any fame or reputation." "No, Basil, you must tell me," murmured Dorian Gray. "I think I have a right to know." His feeling of terror had passed away, and curiosity had taken its place. He was determined to find out Basil Hallward's mystery. "Let us sit down, Dorian," said Hallward, looking pale and pained. "Let us sit down. I will sit in the shadow, and you shall sit in the sunlight. Our lives are like that. Just answer me one question. Have you noticed in the picture something that you did not like?-- something that probably at first did not strike you, but that revealed itself to you suddenly?" "Basil!" cried the lad, clutching the arms of his chair with trembling hands, and gazing at him with wild, startled eyes. "I see you did. Don't speak. Wait till you hear what I have to say. It is quite true that I have worshipped you with far more romance of feeling than a man usually gives to a friend.

original: My one candle seemed suddenly insufficient; the flame flickered, as though in a draft. I forced myself to bend down and retrieve the pile of papers, but they too were a disappointment, for all the drawings were of toys. Here was a ballerina, here a nutcracker, here a tiny gingerbread man. They were as detailed as mechanical drawings, and yet I did not see any clockwork, only strange markings I could not decipher, dimensions and angles that I recognized from no geometry class. A slow wave of darkness passed over my vision, and in it I heard screams, infinite screams, joined together in the wailing wind.

These depictions were as intricate as blueprints for machinery, yet I could discern no internal clockwork mechanisms, only peculiar symbols I was unable to interpret, along with measurements and angles unfamiliar from any geometry lesson. A gradual surge of dimness obscured my sight, and within this obscurity, I perceived shrieks, unending shrieks, interwoven with the howling gale.

original: His neck, with the vertebrae on the outside, rose to an impressionistic approximation of a human head, with startling proportions  cheeks like satellite dishes of moon-cratered bone, closer in form to a tribal war mask than a regular face. His lips were strips of pearl. His eyes were polished white billiard balls. With his mouth closed and his eyes shut, his head seemed more like a modernist ceramic statue than a living creature. Nothing exposed to the elements was flesh and fragile; everything soft was inside. Impermeable to frostbite, he could stroll through the fiercest winter storms  a body that could survive even when entirely encased in ice. His bony arms were latched to the side of his torso, stowed securely rather than hanging idle, released with the accompanying sound of distinct clicks. Rather than sinew and flesh, they were as hard as narwhal tusk. It took enormous self-control for Yotam not to step back as these arms stretched out towards him. Fully extended, the javelin-tip hands divided into digits revealing the most delicate of surgical scalpels, less like human fingers and more like precise medical instruments. With great care, for he could disembowel the blubber belly of a walrus with a flick of a finger, he delicately pressed a digit against Yotams jacket as though aiming for his heart. It was the first time theyd ever touched. Yotam looked down to see that the scalpel tip had punctured the synthetic outer layer so that a single goose feather came free and floated slowly to the ground.
paraphrase: The vertebrae on the exterior of his neck extended upwards, culminating in a head that bore a striking, almost abstract resemblance to a human one, characterized by astonishingly disproportionate features. His cheeks, resembling the concave forms of satellite dishes, were crafted from moon-cratered bone, presenting an appearance more akin to a ceremonial war mask than a typical human countenance. His lips were composed of pearlescent strips, and his eyes gleamed like polished white billiard balls. When his mouth was closed and his eyes were shut, his head conveyed the impression of a contemporary ceramic sculpture rather than that of a living being. No part of him that was exposed to the environment was soft or delicate; all his vulnerable tissues were situated internally. Immune to the effects of frostbite, he possessed the ability to traverse the most intense winter blizzards, his physique capable of enduring even complete encapsulation in ice. His skeletal arms were firmly attached to the sides of his torso, held securely in place rather than dangling loosely, and they released with a series of distinct clicking sounds when moved. Instead of being composed of muscle and skin, these limbs were as unyielding as a narwhal's tusk. Yotam found himself exerting immense self-restraint to avoid recoiling as these arms extended in his direction. With considerable precision, for he possessed the strength to eviscerate the fatty abdomen of a walrus with a mere flick of a finger, he gently pressed one of his digits against Yotam's jacket, as if targeting his heart. This marked the initial physical contact they had ever shared. Yotam glanced downwards to observe that the scalpel-like tip had pierced the synthetic outer layer of his jacket, allowing a solitary goose feather to detach and drift gracefully to the ground.

I conceded that beagles reign supreme among small dogs, but I countered that they aren't truly small dogs. I questioned whether the definition of "small" might vary depending on the individual beagle.

By then, we had arrived at the parking lot and were heading towards Noah's silver Lexus hybrid SUV. "Sugar weighs twenty-five pounds," I stated, emphasizing that twenty-five pounds of such magnificent, glossy, and imposing canine could never be confined to a purse. Noah started to respond, "I'm not disagreeing that Sugar—" but a man's urgent voice cut him off with a shout of, "Hey, Noah!" Instantly, Noah released my hand and softly instructed me, "Keep walking."

original: She took her painfully thin shoulders, felt the trembling in her own bones, and one thought filled her mind. Von Bauer had assaulted her. She should not have brought the girl into this house. Wrong, so wrong of her. She’d been selfish, attempting to provide herself with an ally. Instead, she’d brought devastation into Camille’s young life. Very bad. Then came the soft whimper again. It... But... Vivian! It’s ghastly. Just  awful. Praise God, Jacqueline is safe in the care of her doctor.” For a brief second, something sweet and lovely passed in the girl’s eyes. Then instantly vanished. Her brother, and others like them.” Her eyes filled with a fevered expression, something between terror and panic, but also fury. To a...a death camp.”   “You are sure of this? They were sent to a death camp, not an internment camp?”   “The numbers don’t add up.” She put her head in her hands and spoke through her splayed fingers. No one camp could house so many.”   Such specific information did not come from innocent sources. Vivian couldn’t bear to think what  der Rab  would do if he caught the girl rummaging through his personal correspondence. Even Vivian didn’t dare it. Good. She should be as afraid. She nodded, after all, but she did so absently, and then words were rolling out of her mouth in a rush. It is the Jews they target here.” Camille stared at the floor. When she looked up, her face appeared gaunt and shadowed, as if resistant to light.

It was a mistake to have allowed the girl into this residence. Utterly incorrect, a profound error on her part. She had acted out of self-interest, endeavoring to secure an associate for herself. Instead, she had introduced ruin into Camille’s nascent existence. Exceedingly unfortunate. Subsequently, the faint whimper recurred. It… But… Vivian! This is horrific. Simply dreadful. Thank goodness, Jacqueline is secure under her doctor's supervision.” For a fleeting moment, a gentle and charming quality flickered in the girl's gaze. Then, it immediately vanished. Her brother, and others of their kind.” Her eyes became alight with an agitated look, a fusion of dread and alarm, yet also intense anger. To a...a extermination camp.” “Are you absolutely certain of this? They were dispatched to an extermination camp, not a detainment camp?” “The figures are inconsistent.” She buried her face in her hands and uttered words through her outstretched fingers. No single camp could accommodate such a vast number.” Such precise data did not originate from blameless origins. Vivian could not bear to contemplate the actions der Rab would take if he discovered the girl sifting through his private communications. Not even Vivian dared to do so. Excellent. She ought to be equally apprehensive. She assented, eventually, but she did so distractedly, and then words spilled from her mouth in a torrent. It is the Jewish people they are targeting here.” Camille fixed her gaze on the floor. When she finally raised her head, her face seemed emaciated and shrouded in darkness, as though resisting illumination.

original: A boy at Schulpforta once described for Werner a rally at Nuremberg: an ocean of banners and flags, he said, masses of boys teeming in the lights, and the führer himself on an altar a half mile away, spotlights illuminating pillars behind him, the atmosphere oversaturated with meaning and anger and righteousness, Hans Schilzer crazy for it, Herribert Pomsel crazy for it, every boy at Schulpforta crazy for it, and the only person in Werner’s life who could see through all that stagecraft was his younger sister.
paraphrase: Werner was once told by a boy at Schulpforta about a rally that took place in Nuremberg: a vast expanse of banners and flags, he recounted, with large groups of boys swarming amidst the lights, and the führer positioned on an elevated platform half a mile distant, with spotlights illuminating the columns behind him. The environment was overflowing with significance, fury, and a sense of moral superiority, captivating Hans Schilzer, Herribert Pomsel, and every other boy present at Schulpforta. The sole individual in Werner's existence capable of discerning the artificiality behind all that elaborate presentation was his younger sister.

original: An animal of some kind was prowling across the silvery lawn. Harry dashed to his bedside table, snatched up his glasses, and put them on, then hurried back to the window. It couldn’t be the Grim — not now — not right before the match —  He peered out at the grounds again and, after a minute’s frantic searching, spotted it. It was skirting the edge of the forest now. . . . It wasn’t the Grim at all . . . Harry clutched the window ledge in relief as he recognized the bottlebrush tail. It was only Crookshanks. . . . Or  was  it only Crookshanks? Harry squinted, pressing his nose flat against the glass. Crookshanks seemed to have come to a halt. Harry was sure he could see something else moving in the shadow of the trees too. And just then, it emerged — a gigantic, shaggy black dog, moving stealthily across the lawn, Crookshanks trotting at its side. Harry stared. What did this mean? If Crookshanks could see the dog as well, how could it be an omen of Harry’s death? “Ron!” Harry hissed. Crookshanks and the dog had vanished. Harry climbed onto the windowsill to look right down into the shadows of the castle, but they weren’t there. Where had they gone? A loud snore told him Ron had fallen asleep again. Harry and the rest of the Gryffindor team entered the Great Hall the next day to enormous applause. Harry couldn’t help grinning broadly as he saw that both the Ravenclaw and Hufflepuff tables were applauding them too.

Harry, along with the other members of the Gryffindor team, made their entrance into the Great Hall on the subsequent day to a deafening ovation. Harry found himself unable to suppress a wide smile as he noticed that both the Ravenclaw and Hufflepuff tables were also offering them their applause.

original: He's been frosting those cakes and cookies for years in his family's bakery. But now that he's rich, he can afford to smear real paint on canvases. I don't have a talent, unless you count hunting illegally, which they don't. Or maybe singing, which I wouldn't do for the Capitol in a million years. My mother tried to interest me in a variety of suitable alternatives from a list Effie Trinket sent her. Cooking, flower arranging, playing the flute. None of them took, although Prim had a knack for all three. Finally Cinna stepped in and offered to help me develop my passion for designing clothes, which really required development since it was nonexistent. But I said yes because it meant getting to talk to Cinna, and he promised he'd do all the work. I pick up one of the sketchbooks and examine a dress I supposedly created. Like these. Flowing black pants made of a thick, warm material. A comfortable white shirt. A sweater woven from green and blue and gray strands of kitten-soft wool. Laced leather boots that don't pinch my toes. He hands me a small stack of cards. Try to sound like you care.”       Just then, Effie Trinket arrives in a pumpkin orange wig to remind everyone, “We're on a schedule!” She kisses me on both cheeks while waving in the camera crew, then orders me into position. Effie's the only reason we got anywhere on time in the Capitol, so I try to accommodate her.

For many years, he applied frosting to cakes and cookies at his family's bakery. However, since he has become wealthy, he is now able to apply genuine paint to artistic canvases. I do not possess a particular skill, unless one considers poaching, which is not recognized as such. Alternatively, perhaps singing, an activity I would never perform for the Capitol under any circumstances. My mother made attempts to engage me in several appropriate options from a compilation Effie Trinket had provided her. These included culinary arts, floral arrangements, and playing the flute. None of these pursuits resonated with me, although Prim demonstrated a natural aptitude for all three. Eventually, Cinna intervened and proposed assisting me in cultivating my supposed enthusiasm for fashion design, which genuinely needed fostering given its complete absence. Nevertheless, I agreed because it presented an opportunity to converse with Cinna, and he guaranteed he would handle all the effort. I retrieve one of the sketchbooks and scrutinize a dress that I ostensibly designed. It resembles these: loose-fitting black trousers crafted from a substantial, warm fabric. A pleasant white top. A jumper knitted from interwoven green, blue, and grey threads of exceptionally soft wool. Leather boots with laces that do not constrict my feet. He presents me with a small collection of cards. "Attempt to convey genuine concern."

At that precise moment, Effie Trinket makes her appearance, wearing a pumpkin-orange wig, to announce to everyone, "Our timetable must be adhered to!" She bestows a kiss upon each of my cheeks while gesturing to the camera team, then instructs me to assume my designated place. Effie is the sole individual responsible for our punctuality within the Capitol, so I endeavor to cooperate with her requests.

original: This it did, and was flying down-stream in a current as swift as a mill-race, when Hans checked it with the rope and checked too suddenly. The boat flirted over and snubbed in to the bank bottom up, while Thornton, flung sheer out of it, was carried down-stream toward the worst part of the rapids, a stretch of wild water in which no swimmer could live. Buck had sprung in on the instant; and at the end of three hundred yards, amid a mad swirl of water, he overhauled Thornton. When he felt him grasp his tail, Buck headed for the bank, swimming with all his splendid strength. But the progress shoreward was slow; the progress down-stream amazingly rapid. From below came the fatal roaring where the wild current went wilder and was rent in shreds and spray by the rocks which thrust through like the teeth of an enormous comb. The suck of the water as it took the beginning of the last steep pitch was frightful, and Thornton knew that the shore was impossible. He scraped furiously over a rock, bruised across a second, and struck a third with crushing force. He clutched its slippery top with both hands, releasing Buck, and above the roar of the churning water shouted: "Go, Buck! Go!" Buck could not hold his own, and swept on down-stream, struggling desperately, but unable to win back. When he heard Thornton's command repeated, he partly reared out of the water, throwing his head high, as though for a last look, then turned obediently toward the bank.
paraphrase: It executed this maneuver, moving rapidly down the river in a current that rushed as forcefully as a mill-race, when Hans abruptly halted it using the rope, and did so with excessive suddenness. The vessel flipped over and lodged itself against the bank, keel-up, while Thornton, propelled entirely out of it, was swept downstream towards the most perilous section of the rapids, an expanse of tumultuous water where survival for any swimmer was impossible. Buck immediately plunged in; and after covering three hundred yards, amidst a chaotic vortex of water, he caught up with Thornton. Feeling Thornton seize his tail, Buck oriented himself towards the bank, swimming with the full extent of his magnificent power. However, the movement towards the shore was sluggish; the downstream progression, remarkably swift. From further below, the ominous roar emanated from where the untamed current intensified and was torn into fragments and mist by the protruding rocks, resembling the teeth of a colossal comb. The suction of the water as it commenced the final steep descent was terrifying, and Thornton recognized that reaching the shore was out of the question. He violently scraped against one rock, was battered across another, and impacted a third with devastating force. He clung to its slick summit with both hands, letting go of Buck, and above the clamor of the agitated water, he yelled: "Go, Buck! Go!" Buck found himself unable to maintain his position, and was carried downstream, fighting fiercely, but unable to return. Upon hearing Thornton's directive reiterated, he partially lifted himself out of the water, raising his head high, as if for a final glimpse, then dutifully turned towards the bank.

original: Mr. Windibank draws my interest every quarter and pays it over to mother, and I find that I can do pretty well with what I earn at typewriting. It brings me twopence a sheet, and I can often do from fifteen to twenty sheets in a-day." "You have made your position very clear to me," said Holmes. "This is my friend, Dr. Watson, before whom you can speak as freely as before myself. Kindly tell us now all about your connection with Mr. Hosmer Angel." A flush stole over Miss Sutherland's face, and she picked nervously at the fringe of her jacket.
paraphrase: Every three months, Mr. Windibank collects my interest and gives it to my mother, and I've discovered I manage quite well with my earnings from typewriting. I receive twopence per page, and frequently, I can complete between fifteen and twenty pages within a single day. "Your situation is now perfectly understood by me," Holmes stated. "This gentleman is my associate, Dr. Watson; you may speak with the same candor in his presence as you would in mine. Please proceed to recount the full details concerning your association with Mr. Hosmer Angel." A reddish tint spread across Miss Sutherland's cheeks, and she anxiously fidgeted with the decorative border of her coat.

original: "Three's enough." Piggy's glasses flashed. "I was with him when he found the conch. I was with him before anyone else was." Jack and the others paid no attention. There was a general dispersal. Ralph, Jack and Simon jumped off the platform and walked along the sand past the bathing pool. Piggy hung bumbling behind them. "If Simon walks in the middle of us," said Ralph, "then we could talk over his head." The three of them fell into step. This meant that every now and then Simon had to do a double shuffle to catch up with the others. Presently Ralph stopped and turned back to Piggy. "Look." Jack and Simon pretended to notice nothing. They walked on. "You can't come." Piggy's glasses were misted again--this time with humiliation. "You told 'em. After what I said." His face flushed, his mouth trembled. "After I said I didn't want--" "What on earth are you talking about?" "About being called Piggy. I said I didn't care as long as they didn't call me Piggy; an' I said not to tell and then you went an' said straight out--" Stillness descended on them. Ralph, looking with more understanding at Piggy, saw that he was hurt and crushed. He hovered between the two courses of apology or further insult. "Better Piggy than Fatty," he said at last, with the directness of genuine leadership, "and anyway, I'm sorry if you feel like that. Now go back, Piggy, and take names.
paraphrase: "No more than three," Piggy declared, his spectacles glinting. "I was present when he discovered the conch shell. I was with him even before anyone else." Jack and the rest of the group disregarded his statement. The group dispersed generally. Ralph, Jack, and Simon descended from the platform and proceeded along the beach, passing the natural bathing area. Piggy trailed behind them, somewhat awkwardly. "If Simon positions himself between us," Ralph suggested, "we could converse above his head, so to speak." The trio then adjusted their pace to walk in unison. This arrangement necessitated Simon occasionally performing a quick, shuffling step to keep pace with his companions. Soon after, Ralph paused and turned back to address Piggy. "Listen." Jack and Simon feigned unawareness, continuing their walk. "You are not permitted to join us." Piggy's glasses had once again fogged over, this time due to profound embarrassment. "You revealed it to them. Despite what I specifically requested." His complexion reddened, and his lips quivered. "After I explicitly stated I didn't desire--" "What exactly are you referring to?" "Regarding being referred to as Piggy. I communicated that I was indifferent as long as they refrained from calling me Piggy; and I specifically requested that you not disclose it, and yet you proceeded to announce it directly--" A hush fell upon them. Ralph, observing Piggy with increased empathy, perceived his wounded and defeated state. He deliberated between offering an apology or inflicting further offense. "It's preferable to be called Piggy than Fatty," he finally stated, exhibiting the straightforwardness characteristic of authentic leadership, "and regardless, I apologize if you feel that way. Now, return, Piggy, and record the names."

original: She opened her flashlight app and shined it in front of her, noting that August was right. The stairs were treacherous. But they were dry and the handrail wasnt rusted as it was outside. She felt safe enough to venture inside, descending slowly into the underground. Natalie . . .  August said thickly. Hold on. I think I should go first. I promise you, its okay. Im not scared of a bat or two. Bats? Sure. They love caves. You could have a whole colony down here Youre going too fast. Slow down. Ignoring his odd tone, Natalie swung the flashlight left and revealed a long, oval-shaped room.
paraphrase: With a flick of her wrist, she activated her flashlight application, directing its beam forward. She observed that August's assessment was accurate: the stairway was indeed hazardous. However, the stairs were arid, and the handrail, unlike its exterior counterpart, showed no signs of corrosion. This provided her with sufficient reassurance to proceed indoors, cautiously making her way down into the subterranean area. "Natalie..." August uttered with a strained voice. "Wait. I believe I should lead. I assure you, everything is fine. I am not intimidated by a few bats." "Bats? Of course. They are fond of caverns. You could have an entire group of them dwelling here." "You are moving too rapidly. Reduce your speed." Disregarding his peculiar intonation, Natalie swept the flashlight beam to the left, illuminating an expansive, elongated, oval-shaped chamber.

original: But his personality changed drastically. Where he once was an even-keeled, decent man, after his recovery he was prone to swearing, quick to anger, and impulsive. Because that particular part of his brain was damaged? Exactly. Its amazing he lived at all. Peter added pasta to the water once it was boiling, and when it was ready, he spooned it into two bowls, followed by tomato sauce and a sprinkle of basil. Marion carried the bowls over to the dining room table. She had to move some papers to make room, and a familiar grid caught her eye. You do crosswords? she asked. He joined her at the table and nodded. Thats one Im working on from the Times. I do the Sunday one, but only in pencil. Marions father worked religiously on that crossword every Sunday morning after breakfast. Marion had only taken it up since shed started dancing as a Rockette. There was something comforting about knowing they were probably working on it at the same moment, Simon in his easy chair and Marion at her dressing table. I always have to erase and it ends up being a mess, but so far Ive been able to finish. She was quite proud of that fact. Peter wiped his mouth with his napkin. I actually create the puzzle, then send it to the Times and hope they use it. So far, theyve published three of mine. Was there anything this guy couldnt do? You are multitalented, clearly.
paraphrase: His demeanor underwent a dramatic transformation. Previously, he had been a calm and respectable individual, but following his recuperation, he became susceptible to profanity, easily provoked, and impetuous. Was this due to damage in that specific region of his brain? Precisely. It’s remarkable that he survived at all. Peter introduced pasta into the water once it reached a rolling boil, and upon its readiness, he portioned it into two dishes, subsequently adding tomato sauce and a dusting of basil. Marion transported the dishes to the dining room table. She needed to relocate some documents to clear a space, and a recognizable grid pattern caught her attention. Do you engage in crossword puzzles? she inquired. He joined her at the table and affirmed with a nod. That’s one I’m currently solving from the Times. I tackle the Sunday edition, but exclusively with a pencil. Marion’s father diligently worked on that particular crossword every Sunday morning after breakfast. Marion had only adopted the hobby since she commenced her career as a Rockette dancer. There was a certain solace in the thought that they were likely engaged in the same activity simultaneously, Simon in his comfortable armchair and Marion at her vanity. I invariably need to make corrections, and it often becomes untidy, yet I’ve consistently managed to complete it. She felt a considerable sense of accomplishment regarding this achievement. Peter dabbed his mouth with his napkin. In fact, I devise the puzzle, then submit it to the Times with the hope that they will feature it. To date, they have published three of my creations. Was there any skill this man lacked? You possess multiple talents, undoubtedly.

original: Squeeze! squeeze! squeeze! all the morning long; I squeezed that sperm till I myself almost melted into it; I squeezed that sperm till a strange sort of insanity came over me; and I found myself unwittingly squeezing my co-laborers' hands in it, mistaking their hands for the gentle globules. Such an abounding, affectionate, friendly, loving feeling did this avocation beget; that at last I was continually squeezing their hands, and looking up into their eyes sentimentally; as much as to say, --Oh! my dear fellow beings, why should we longer cherish any social acerbities, or know the slightest ill-humor or envy! Come; let us squeeze hands all round; nay, let us all squeeze ourselves .
paraphrase: All through the morning hours, I was engaged in a continuous act of squeezing! I pressed that sperm with such intensity that I nearly felt myself dissolving into it. The relentless squeezing of that sperm eventually led to a peculiar kind of derangement taking hold of me. In this state, I inadvertently began pressing the hands of my fellow workers, confusing their hands for the soft, rounded masses I had been manipulating. This particular task fostered such an overflowing, tender, amiable, and fond emotion within me that, ultimately, I was persistently clasping their hands and gazing into their eyes with deep sentiment, as if to communicate, "Oh! My beloved companions, why should we continue to harbor any social resentments, or experience even the faintest displeasure or jealousy! Come now; let us all join hands in a squeeze; in fact, let us all embrace each other in a collective squeeze."

original: " "My brother is very anxious to have the Hall inhabited, for he thinks it is for the good of the poor folk upon the moor. He would be very angry if he knew that I have said anything which might induce Sir Henry to go away. But I have done my duty now and I will say no more. I must go back, or he will miss me and suspect that I have seen you. Good-bye!" She turned and had disappeared in a few minutes among the scattered boulders, while I, with my soul full of vague fears, pursued my way to Baskerville Hall.
paraphrase: " "My sibling is quite eager to see the Hall occupied, as he believes it will benefit the impoverished people living on the moor. He would be furious if he discovered I uttered anything that might persuade Sir Henry to depart. However, I have fulfilled my obligation, and I shall speak no further. I need to return, otherwise, he will notice my absence and suspect I have met with you. Farewell!" She spun around and vanished within moments amidst the dispersed rocks, while I, my spirit filled with undefined apprehensions, continued my journey toward Baskerville Hall.

original: “I wish to say this. Our unknown friend accuses me of the murder of one Edward Seton. I remember Seton perfectly well. He came up before me for trial in June of the year 1930. He was charged with the murder of an elderly woman. He was very ably defended and made a good impression on the jury in the witness-box. Nevertheless, on the evidence, he was certainly guilty. I summed up accordingly, and the jury brought in a verdict of Guilty. In passing sentence of death I concurred with the verdict. An appeal was lodged on the grounds of misdirection. The appeal was rejected and the man was duly executed. I wish to say before you all that my con science is perfectly clear on the matter. I did my duty and nothing more. I passed sentence on a rightly convicted murderer.”
paraphrase: "I want to make this statement. The individual whose identity is unknown to us is accusing me of being responsible for the death of a man named Edward Seton. I recall Seton quite distinctly. He appeared before me in court for his trial during June of the year 1930. The charge against him was the killing of an older woman. His defense was conducted with great skill, and he presented himself favorably to the jury while giving his testimony. However, based on the presented evidence, there was no doubt he was guilty. My summary of the case reflected this, and the jury subsequently delivered a verdict of Guilty. When pronouncing the death sentence, I agreed with their decision. An appeal was filed, citing an error in the judge's instructions to the jury. This appeal was denied, and the man was subsequently put to death as prescribed by law. I want to declare in front of everyone present that my conscience is entirely untroubled regarding this situation. I fulfilled my obligations and nothing beyond that. I imposed a sentence upon a murderer who was rightfully convicted."

An appeal was filed, citing an error in the judge's instructions to the jury. This appeal was denied, and the man was subsequently put to death as prescribed by law. I want to declare in front of everyone present that my conscience is entirely untroubled regarding this situation. I fulfilled my obligations and nothing beyond that. I imposed a sentence upon a murderer who was rightfully convicted.\"", "sps": 0.9223002195358276, "wordsim": 0.21354121891166314, "final_score": 0.7087590006241645}]

original: In a vague way he understood that his father was in trouble and that this was the reason why he himself had not been sent back to Clongowes. For some time he had felt the slight change in his house; and those changes in what he had deemed unchangeable were so many slight shocks to his boyish conception of the world. The ambition which he felt astir at times in the darkness of his soul sought no outlet. A dusk like that of the outer world obscured his mind as he heard the mare's hoofs clattering along the tramtrack on the Rock Road and the great can swaying and rattling behind him. He returned to Mercedes and, as he brooded upon her image, a strange unrest crept into his blood. Sometimes a fever gathered within him and led him to rove alone in the evening along the quiet avenue. The peace of the gardens and the kindly lights in the windows poured a tender influence into his restless heart. The noise of children at play annoyed him and their silly voices made him feel, even more keenly than he had felt at Clongowes, that he was different from others. He did not want to play. He wanted to meet in the real world the unsubstantial image which his soul so constantly beheld. He did not know where to seek it or how, but a premonition which led him on told him that this image would, without any overt act of his, encounter him.

For a period, he had perceived a subtle shift within his home; these alterations to what he had considered immutable delivered numerous minor disturbances to his youthful perception of the universe. The aspiration that occasionally stirred in the depths of his spirit found no means of expression. A gloom, akin to that enveloping the external environment, clouded his thoughts as he listened to the horse's hooves clatter on the tramlines of the Rock Road and the substantial cart rocking and rattling in his wake. He turned his attention back to Mercedes, and as he contemplated her likeness, an unfamiliar disquietude began to permeate his veins. Occasionally, an intense agitation would well up within him, prompting him to wander by himself in the evenings along the tranquil thoroughfare. The serenity of the green spaces and the gentle illumination from the windows infused a comforting sensation into his agitated spirit. The commotion of children engaged in games irritated him, and their childish vocalizations made him acutely aware, even more so than during his time at Clongowes, of his divergence from others. He had no desire to participate in play. His yearning was to encounter, in the tangible world, the ethereal representation that his inner self perpetually envisioned. He was uncertain of its whereabouts or the method of its discovery, yet an intuitive foreboding that guided him indicated that this vision would, without any direct effort on his part, cross his path.
