original: Suddenly I saw the Hispaniola in front of me about half a mile away. But the ship was moving strangely. She sailed and stopped, sailed and stopped; and she turned north, south, east, west. I watched her with surprise. And then I understood! The two pirates were drunk or asleep. I had an idea: 'Perhaps I can go on board and take her to the captain.' So I paddled towards her, and when I came near to her, I jumped on to the bowsprit above me. My boat went under the water. But now I was on the Hispaniola!   Was there anybody on board? I heard only the sound of the ship when she turned in the wind. Then I saw the two pirates on deck. Perhaps they were asleep. But when I saw some blood on the deck, I was sure they were dead. Then Israel Hands moved. There were lots of empty bottles. I found one with some brandy, and I took some biscuits and cheese and went up on deck. I drank a lot of water from the water barrel and then I gave the brandy to Hands. He drank a lot quickly. When I took down the black pirate flag, he said, 'Shall we talk, Captain Hawkins? Me and this man here - O'Brien - we wanted to sail the ship back to the harbour, where she was before. Who is going to sail her? You? No, you can't do it. Give me food and drink, and something for my wound.
paraphrase: Suddenly, approximately half a mile ahead, the Hispaniola appeared in my sight. However, the vessel's movement was erratic. She would advance and halt, advance and halt; furthermore, she veered towards the north, then south, then east, and finally west. I observed her with astonishment. At that moment, comprehension dawned on me! The pair of pirates were either inebriated or unconscious. An idea struck me: "Perhaps I can board her and bring her to the captain." Consequently, I propelled my small boat in her direction, and upon nearing the Hispaniola, I leaped onto the bowsprit situated above me. My boat submerged. But now I had successfully boarded the Hispaniola! Was there anyone else present on the ship? The only sound I detected was that of the ship as it shifted with the wind. Subsequently, I spotted the two pirates on the deck. It was possible they were sleeping. Yet, upon noticing some blood staining the deck, I became convinced they were deceased. Then, Israel Hands stirred. Numerous empty bottles lay scattered about. I discovered one containing brandy, and I gathered some biscuits and cheese before ascending to the deck. I consumed a substantial amount of water from the water barrel and then offered the brandy to Hands. He consumed a large quantity rapidly. When I lowered the black pirate flag, he uttered, "Shall we have a discussion, Captain Hawkins? This man here – O'Brien – and I, we intended to navigate the ship back to the harbor, its previous location. Who will be sailing her? You? No, you are incapable of it. Provide me with sustenance and drink, and something for my injury."

original: It got to be terrible in the store. The more his back ached and his muscle dissolved into fat and the fat melted off his bones, the more fractious he became with Janie. Especially in the store. The more people in there the more ridicule he poured over her body to point attention away from his own. So one day Steve Mixon wanted some chewing tobacco and Janie cut it wrong.
paraphrase: The shop became an unbearable place. As his back pain worsened, his muscles turned to fat, and that fat wasted away from his frame, his behavior towards Janie grew increasingly irritable. This was particularly true within the store's confines. With each additional customer present, he intensified his mockery of her physical appearance, seeking to divert scrutiny from himself. Consequently, on a particular day, when Steve Mixon requested chewing tobacco, Janie made an error in cutting it.

For some months he had been engaged to a young servant-woman, waiting only for a little increase to their mutual savings in order to their marriage; and it was a great delight to him that Sarah did not object to William’s occasional presence in their Sunday interviews.
paraphrase: Silas, in his naivete, believed their friendship had not been diminished, even after he developed a more intimate relationship. It brought him much joy that Sarah raised no objections to William joining them sometimes during their Sunday meetings.

Well do everything we can to ensure a smooth transition. Your patients, your medical staff, will be the first to go over  along with any necessary medical equipment and supplies, I said, keeping my voice firm but tinged with regret. Viv shook her head, her frustration turning into desperation. Dammit, I dont want to move those patients. I continued to stare at her. Sure, I heard her words, but they werent ringing true.
paraphrase: Her assessment was accurate. HealthBay accommodated numerous injured pilots, utilizing specialized equipment and medical provisions stored within its compartments. "This undertaking will present considerable challenges, yet we will accomplish it. We will exert every effort to guarantee a seamless transfer. Your patients and your medical personnel will be the initial ones to relocate, accompanied by any essential medical apparatus and provisions," I declared, my tone resolute yet tinged with a sense of sorrow. Viv shook her head, her exasperation escalating into a state of despair. "Damn it, I have no desire to relocate those patients." I maintained my unwavering gaze upon her. Indeed, I registered her statement, but it lacked authenticity.

original: No one paid any attention to the two travelers. When men are at war with one another, the Soul of the World can hear the screams of battle. No one fails to suffer the consequences of everything under the sun.”   All things are one, the boy thought. And then, as if the desert wanted to demonstrate that the alchemist was right, two horsemen appeared from behind the travelers. They were silent for a moment, and then agreed that the boy and the alchemist could move along. He had noticed that, in the midst of the multitude of armed men back at the encampment, there had been one who stared fixedly at the two. He had been so far away that his face wasn’t even visible. But the boy was certain that he had been looking at them. It is about penetrating to the Soul of the World, and discovering the treasure that has been reserved for you.”   “No, that’s not what I mean. I’m talking about transforming lead into gold.”   The alchemist fell as silent as the desert, and answered the boy only after they had stopped to eat. Don’t ask me why; I don’t know why. I just know that the Tradition is always right. So gold, instead of being seen as a symbol of evolution, became the basis for conflict.”   “There are many languages spoken by things,” the boy said. Then it became a signal of danger. And, finally, it became just a whinny again.”   But then he stopped.
paraphrase: The pair of travelers went unnoticed. During times of human conflict, the universal spirit, known as the Soul of the World, is able to perceive the cries of combat. Everyone, without exception, experiences the repercussions of every event that occurs on Earth." The boy mused, "All things are interconnected." Just then, as if to confirm the alchemist's assertion, two horsemen emerged from behind the duo. They remained quiet for a moment before consenting to allow the boy and the alchemist to proceed. He had observed that, among the numerous armed individuals at the encampment earlier, one person had been staring intently at them. This individual was so distant that their facial features were indistinguishable. Nevertheless, the boy felt certain that this person had been observing them. It involves delving into the Soul of the World and unearthing the unique fortune that awaits you." "No, that's not my intention. I'm referring to the process of transmuting lead into gold." The alchemist became as quiet as the desert itself, only responding to the boy after they had paused for a meal. "Do not inquire as to the reason; I am unaware of it. I simply recognize that the Tradition is unfailingly correct. Consequently, gold, rather than being perceived as a representation of development, transformed into the root of contention." The boy remarked, "Many things communicate through various forms of expression." Subsequently, it evolved into a warning sign. And, ultimately, it reverted to being merely a whinny once more." However, he then ceased speaking.

original: They’d all reassured me; Alice reaching up to pat my head as I left, eyeing Jasper meaningfully until a flood of peace swirled around me, Esme kissing my forehead and promising me everything was all right, Emmett laughing boisterously and asking why I was the only one who was allowed to fight with werewolves . . . Jacob’s solution had them all relaxed, almost euphoric after the long weeks of stress. Doubt had been replaced with confidence. The party had ended on a note of true celebration.

The gathering concluded with a genuine sense of festivity.

original: "Scum!" Feyd-Rautha gasped. At the key word, the gladiator's muscles obeyed with a momentary slackness. It was enough for Feyd-Rautha. He opened a space between them sufficient for the long blade. Its poisoned tip flicked out, drew a red line down the slave's chest. There was instant agony in the poison. The man disengaged himself, staggered backward. Now, let my dear family watch, Feyd-Rautha thought. Let them think on this slave who tried to turn the knife he thought poisoned and use it against me. Let them wonder how a gladiator could come into this arena ready for such an attempt.
paraphrase: "Filth!" Feyd-Rautha exhaled sharply. Upon hearing the specific command, the warrior's muscles briefly relaxed. This brief hesitation was all Feyd-Rautha needed. He swiftly created enough distance between himself and the gladiator to deploy the extended knife. The venomous point of the blade darted forward, leaving a crimson mark across the slave's torso. The poison inflicted immediate, searing pain. The man recoiled, stumbling away from the confrontation. "Now, let my beloved relatives witness this," Feyd-Rautha mused internally. "Let them reflect on this slave who attempted to reverse the supposedly poisoned weapon and employ it against me. Let them ponder how a gladiator could enter this stadium prepared for such an audacious act."

original: Theyre probably smoking cigars in there. Maud texted Ella again. Im fine MOM! With a final blast of his horn, the clown waved goodbye, shift finished. Still holding her kite, Louise took over leading the group. The other children followed her down the beach, skirting the clutching fingers of tide toward a cluster of objects that, Maud saw as they all drew nearer, werent rocks or washed-up redwood trunks as they had seemed. Three baby sea lions, ribs pushing though their mottled skin, eyes dull, lifted their heads, squirmed, dropped back down to the sand. Theyre starving, someone said. Poor things. In a chorus of alarmed exclamations, the children reeled in their kites. A woman called a ranger on her phone and alerted the fathers, several of whom now materialized on the beach. Climate change, a woman said. They come in too far to find food. We need to go back to Ella, Maud told Louise. We cant leave them, Louise said. She had tears in her eyes. Cant we do something? Like carry them back into the water? Lets wait for the experts, Maud said. She texted Peter, who was still inside, and he replied that hed take a cab home to Ella. Finally, two rangers arrived. They loaded the sea lions onto stretchers, explaining that theyd release them back into the water when they were strong enough to swim again. The other youngsters trailed her along the beach, navigating around the grasping tendrils of the tide toward a collection of items that, as Maud observed upon their closer approach, were neither rocks nor fallen redwood logs as they initially appeared. Three young sea lions, their ribs visibly protruding through their dappled hides, and their eyes lacking luster, raised their heads, wriggled, then settled back onto the sand. "They're famished," someone remarked. "Poor creatures." Amidst a symphony of distressed cries, the children retracted their kites. A woman contacted a park ranger on her mobile phone and informed the fathers, several of whom promptly appeared on the beach. "Climate change," a woman declared. "They venture too far inland in search of nourishment." "We must return to Ella," Maud instructed Louise. "We cannot abandon them," Louise responded, her eyes welling with tears. She messaged Peter, who remained indoors, and he confirmed he would take a taxi home to Ella. Eventually, two park rangers arrived. They carefully placed the sea lions onto stretchers, clarifying that they would release them back into the sea once they had regained sufficient strength to swim independently. As the vehicle departed, the children applauded, with the exception of Louise, who remained silent during the stroll to the automobile.

original: Her heart jumped painfully within her. The priestess had now reached Okonkwo's compound and was talking with him outside his hut. She was saying again and again that Agbala wanted to see his daughter, Ezinma. Okonkwo pleaded with her to come back in the morning because Ezinma was now asleep. But Chielo ignored what he was trying to say and went on shouting that Agbala wanted to see his daughter. Her voice was as clear as metal, and Okonkwo's women and children heard from their huts all that she said. Okonkwo was still pleading that the girl had been ill of late and was asleep. Ekwefi quickly took her to their bedroom and placed her on their high bamboo bed. The priestess screamed. "Beware, Okonkwo!" she warned. "Beware of exchanging words with Agbala. Does a man speak when a god speaks? Beware!" She walked through Okonkwo's hut into the circular compound and went straight toward Ekwefi's hut. Okonkwo came after her. "Ekwefi," she called, "Agbala greets you. Where is my daughter, Ezinma? Agbala wants to see her." Ekwefi came out from her hut carrying her oil lamp in her left hand. There was a light wind blowing, so she cupped her right hand to shelter the flame. Nwoye's mother, also carrying an oil lamp, emerged from her hut. The children stood in the darkness outside their hut watching the strange event. Okonkwo's youngest wife also came out and joined the others.
paraphrase: Inside her, her heart gave a painful lurch. The priestess had, by this point, arrived at Okonkwo's compound and was engaged in conversation with him just outside his dwelling. She was reiterating, repeatedly, that Agbala desired an audience with his daughter, Ezinma. Okonkwo implored her to return at daybreak, explaining that Ezinma was currently in slumber. However, Chielo disregarded his attempts to communicate and persisted in proclaiming loudly that Agbala wished to see his daughter. Her voice possessed the sharpness of metal, and Okonkwo's wives and children, from within their respective huts, overheard every word she uttered. Okonkwo continued to plead, emphasizing that the girl had been unwell recently and was now sleeping. Swiftly, Ekwefi led her into their sleeping quarters and positioned her on their elevated bamboo sleeping platform. The priestess shrieked. "Be careful, Okonkwo!" she cautioned. "Be careful about arguing with Agbala. Does a mortal speak when a deity speaks? Be careful!" She proceeded through Okonkwo's hut, entering the round courtyard, and moved directly towards Ekwefi's hut. Okonkwo followed behind her. "Ekwefi," she called out, "Agbala sends his greetings. Where is my daughter, Ezinma? Agbala requests her presence." Ekwefi emerged from her hut, an oil lamp held in her left hand. A gentle breeze was stirring, so she formed a cup with her right hand to shield the flame. Nwoye's mother, also carrying an oil lamp, appeared from her own hut. The children remained in the darkness outside their hut, observing the unusual occurrence. Okonkwo's youngest spouse also came out and joined the rest of the group.

original: He didn’t care if you’d packed something or not and had it way in the top of the closet. I got them for him though. I nearly got killed doing it, too. The second I opened the closet door, Stradlater’s tennis racket—in its wooden press and all—fell right on my head. It made a big clunk, and it hurt like hell. It damn near killed old Ackley, though.
paraphrase: He was completely indifferent to whether you had packed something or not, even if it was stashed deep inside the closet. Despite that, I retrieved them for him. I almost lost my life in the process, as well. The very moment I unlatched the closet door, Stradlater's tennis racket, still secured within its wooden press, plummeted directly onto my skull. It produced a loud thudding sound, and the pain was excruciating. However, it very nearly fatally injured poor Ackley.

original: Tookey, the deputy-clerk, who shared the unpopularity common to deputies, turned very red, but replied, with careful moderation—“Mr. Winthrop, if you’ll bring me any proof as I’m in the wrong, I’m not the man to say I won’t alter. But there’s people set up their own ears for a standard, and expect the whole choir to follow ’em. There may be two opinions, I hope.”     “Aye, aye,” said Mr. Macey, who felt very well satisfied with this attack on youthful presumption; “you’re right there, Tookey: there’s allays two ’pinions; there’s the ’pinion a man has of himsen, and there’s the ’pinion other folks have on him. There’d be two ’pinions about a cracked bell, if the bell could hear itself.”     “Well, Mr. Macey,” said poor Tookey, serious amidst the general laughter, “I undertook to partially fill up the office of parish-clerk by Mr. Crackenthorp’s desire, whenever your infirmities should make you unfitting; and it’s one of the rights thereof to sing in the choir—else why have you done the same yourself?”     “Ah! but the old gentleman and you are two folks,” said Ben Winthrop. Why, the Squire used to invite him to take a glass, only to hear him sing the “Red Rovier”; didn’t he, Mr. Macey? It’s a nat’ral gift. There’s my little lad Aaron, he’s got a gift—he can sing a tune off straight, like a throstle. But as for you, Master Tookey, you’d better stick to your “Amens”: your voice is well enough when you keep it up in your nose.
paraphrase:
Tookey, the deputy-clerk, who experienced the widespread disfavor often associated with deputies, became noticeably flushed but responded with deliberate restraint: "Mr. Winthrop, should you present me with any evidence proving my error, I am not one to refuse to change my stance. However, some individuals establish their own judgment as the definitive measure, anticipating that everyone else will conform. I trust that differing perspectives are permissible."

"Indeed, indeed," affirmed Mr. Macey, clearly pleased by this critique of youthful arrogance; "you are correct, Tookey: there are perpetually two viewpoints; there is a man's perception of himself, and then there is the perception others hold of him. Even a damaged bell would elicit two opinions, if it possessed the ability to hear itself."

"Nevertheless, Mr. Macey," poor Tookey interjected, earnest amid the collective amusement, "I agreed to assume the duties of parish-clerk on a partial basis at Mr. Crackenthorp's request, whenever your ailments rendered you incapable; and a privilege inherent to that role is singing in the choir—otherwise, why did you engage in the same activity?"

"Ah! but the venerable gentleman and you are distinct individuals," observed Ben Winthrop. "Why, the Squire would frequently invite him for a drink, solely to listen to him sing the 'Red Rovier'; isn't that right, Mr. Macey? It's an innate talent. My young son Aaron, for instance, possesses a gift—he can sing a melody flawlessly, much like a thrush. But as for you, Master Tookey, it would be prudent for you to confine yourself to your 'Amens': your voice is adequate only when you project it nasally."

original: It was quite spotless in its cleanliness. The old-fashioned brass knocker on the low arched door, ornamented with carved garlands of fruit and flowers, twinkled like a star; the two stone steps descending to the door were as white as if they had been covered with fair linen; and all the angles and corners, and carvings and mouldings, and quaint little panes of glass, and quainter little windows, though as old as the hills, were as pure as any snow that ever fell upon the hills. When the pony-chaise stopped at the door, and my eyes were intent upon the house, I saw a cadaverous face appear at a small window on the ground floor (in a little round tower that formed one side of the house), and quickly disappear. The low arched door then opened, and the face came out. It was quite as cadaverous as it had looked in the window, though in the grain of it there was that tinge of red which is sometimes to be observed in the skins of red-haired people. It belonged to a red-haired person - a youth of fifteen, as I take it now, but looking much older - whose hair was cropped as close as the closest stubble; who had hardly any eyebrows, and no eyelashes, and eyes of a red-brown, so unsheltered and unshaded, that I remember wondering how he went to sleep. He was high-shouldered and bony; dressed in decent black, with a white wisp of a neckcloth; buttoned up to the throat; and had a long, lank, skeleton hand, which particularly attracted my attention, as he stood at the pony's head, rubbing his chin with it, and looking up at us in the chaise.
paraphrase: The immaculate condition of the place was striking. The antique brass knocker, adorning the gracefully arched low door, embellished with intricate carvings of garlands featuring fruits and blossoms, gleamed brilliantly, reminiscent of a celestial body. The pair of stone steps leading down to the entrance appeared pristine, as though draped in unblemished white linen. Every sharp edge and recess, every decorative carving and molding, and each charmingly small pane of glass within the even more peculiar tiny windows, despite their ancient appearance, possessed a purity comparable to freshly fallen snow on a mountaintop. As the pony-chaise came to a halt in front of the door, and my gaze was fixed on the dwelling, I noticed a gaunt face materialize briefly at a small window on the ground floor, situated within a small circular tower that constituted one section of the house, before promptly vanishing. Subsequently, the low, arched door swung open, and the aforementioned face emerged. It was every bit as gaunt as it had appeared through the window, though upon closer inspection, it exhibited a reddish hue sometimes present in the complexions of individuals with red hair. The face belonged to a person with red hair—a young man, whom I now estimate to be about fifteen years old, but who then seemed considerably older. His hair was cut extremely short, resembling the closest-cropped stubble. He possessed almost no eyebrows and lacked eyelashes entirely. His eyes were a reddish-brown, so exposed and lacking in shading that I distinctly recall pondering how he managed to fall asleep. He had prominent shoulders and a lean, bony frame. He was attired in respectable black clothing, complemented by a slender white neckcloth, and buttoned all the way up to his throat. He also had a long, thin, skeletal hand, which particularly captured my notice as he stood by the pony's head, using it to rub his chin while looking up at us from the chaise.

original: But I rubbed it out anyway, ﬁnally. Then I went on up to the principal’s oﬃce. I told her I was Phoebe Caulﬁeld’s brother, in 4B-1, and I asked her to please give Phoebe the note. I said it was very important because my mother was sick and wouldn’t have lunch ready for Phoebe and that she’d have to meet me and have lunch in a drugstore. She was very nice about it, the old lady. She took the note oﬀ me and called some other lady, from the next oﬃce, and the other lady went to give it to Phoebe. Then the old lady that was around a hundred years old and I shot the breeze for a while, She was pretty nice, and I told her how I’d gone there to school, too, and my brothers. She asked me where I went to school now, and I told her Pencey, and she said Pencey was a very good school. Even if I’d wanted to, I wouldn’t have had the strength to straighten her out. Besides, if she thought Pencey was a very good school, let her think it. You hate to tell new stuﬀ to somebody around a hundred years old. They don’t like to hear it. Then, after a while, I left. It was funny. She yelled “Good luck!” at me the same way old Spencer did when I left Pencey. God, how I hate it when somebody yells “Good luck!” at me when I’m leaving somewhere.
paraphrase: I ultimately erased it, despite my initial hesitation. Following that, I proceeded directly to the principal's office. I informed her that I was the brother of Phoebe Caulfield, who was in class 4B-1, and politely requested that she deliver a note to Phoebe for me. I emphasized its critical nature, explaining that our mother was unwell and unable to prepare Phoebe's lunch, necessitating that Phoebe meet me to eat at a drugstore. The principal, an elderly woman, was exceedingly kind about the situation. She accepted the note from me and summoned another woman from the adjacent office, who then took the note to Phoebe. Subsequently, the principal, who appeared to be approximately a century old, and I engaged in some casual conversation. She was quite pleasant, and I mentioned that I, along with my brothers, had also attended that school previously. She inquired about my current educational institution, to which I responded "Pencey," and she remarked that Pencey was an excellent school. Even if I had harbored the desire, I lacked the energy to correct her perception. Moreover, if she held the belief that Pencey was an excellent school, I saw no reason to disabuse her of that notion. One generally avoids introducing novel or unpleasant information to individuals who are roughly a hundred years old, as they typically do not appreciate hearing it. After a period, I departed. A peculiar thing occurred; she shouted, “Good luck!” to me, echoing the exact sentiment old Spencer had expressed when I left Pencey. I find it intensely irritating when someone wishes me “Good luck!” as I am departing a place.

original: "Eh!" he said. "I was up long before him. How could I have stayed abed! Th' world's all fair begun again this mornin', it has. An' it's workin' an' hummin' an' scratchin' an' pipin' an' nest-buildin' an' breathin' out scents, till you've got to be out on it 'stead o' lyin' on your back. When th' sun did jump up, th' moor went mad for joy, an' I was in the midst of th' heather, an' I run like mad myself, shoutin' an' singin'. An' I come straight here. I couldn't have stayed away. Why, th' garden was lyin' here waitin'!" Mary put her hands on her chest, panting, as if she had been running herself.
paraphrase: "Good heavens!" he exclaimed. "I woke much earlier than he did. How could I possibly have remained in bed? The world has truly started anew and beautifully this morning, indeed. And it's bustling and buzzing and digging and chirping and constructing nests and emitting fragrances, to the point where you simply must be outdoors experiencing it instead of reclining idly. When the sun finally appeared, the moor erupted with happiness, and I was right there amidst the heather, and I dashed about like a madman myself, yelling and singing. And I arrived directly at this spot. I found it impossible to stay away. Why, the garden was right here, anticipating my arrival!" Mary placed her hands on her chest, breathing heavily, as if she herself had just finished running.

original: Ha! explain!" commanded the lady.     "Pardon me, madam; no need of explanation; your own fine sense must inform you that one of your frowns would be a sufficient substitute for capital punishment."     "Sing!" said she, and again touching the piano she commenced an accompaniment in spirited style.     "Now is my time to slip away," thought I; but the tones that then severed the air arrested me. Mrs. Fairfax had said Mr. Rochester possessed a fine voice; he did a mellow, powerful bass, into which he threw his own feeling, his own force; finding a way through the ear to the heart, and there waking sensation strangely. I waited till the last deep and full vibration had expired till the tide of talk, checked an instant, had resumed its flow; I then quitted my sheltered corner and made my exit by the sidedoor, which was fortunately near. Thence a narrow passage led into the hall; in crossing it, I perceived my sandal was loose; I stopped to tie it, kneeling down for that purpose on the mat at the foot of the staircase. I heard the dining-room door unclose; a gentleman came out; rising hastily, I stood face to face with him it was Mr. Rochester.     "How do you do?" he asked.     "I am very well, sir.
paraphrase:
"Ha! Explain yourself!" the woman demanded.

"Forgive me, madam; an explanation isn't necessary; your keen perception should make it clear to you that even a single disapproving glance from you would be more than enough to replace a death sentence," he replied.

"Sing!" she instructed, and once more she made contact with the piano keys, starting an energetic musical accompaniment.

"This is my chance to discreetly leave," I mused; however, the musical notes that then cut through the atmosphere stopped me in my tracks. Mrs. Fairfax had mentioned that Mr. Rochester had an excellent singing voice; indeed, he possessed a rich, resonant bass voice, into which he poured his personal emotions and his own intensity; it found a path through the auditory sense directly to the core of one's being, and there it evoked peculiar feelings. I remained until the final profound and complete resonance had faded away—until the stream of conversation, briefly interrupted, had restarted its progression; I then departed from my secluded spot and exited through the side entrance, which, serendipitously, was close by. From there, a slender corridor led into the main hall; as I traversed it, I noticed that my sandal was untied; I paused to secure it, kneeling down on the rug at the base of the staircase for that specific reason. I heard the dining-room portal open; a male individual emerged; getting up quickly, I found myself directly confronting him—it was Mr. Rochester.

"How are you faring?" he inquired.

"I am quite well, sir," I responded.

original: ‘Where is he?’ he inquired. And as he spoke he saw a young man coming round the corner of the house between two dragoons. He had a long thin neck, and his head, that had been half shaved, was again covered by short hair. This young man was dressed in a threadbare blue cloth coat lined with fox fur, that had once been smart, and dirty hempen convict trousers, over which were pulled his thin, dirty, trodden-down boots.
paraphrase: “Where is he?” he asked. And just as he uttered these words, he observed a youthful man approaching from around the bend of the residence, flanked by two dragoons. This individual possessed an elongated, slender neck, and his head, which had previously been partially shorn, was now once more adorned with short hair. The young man was clad in a worn-out blue woolen coat, lined with fox fur, which had evidently seen better days, and soiled convict trousers made of hemp, beneath which were drawn his slender, grimy, and worn-out boots.

original: Rogers, summoned before the court, had very little to tell. He had been busy all the morning about household duties and with the preparation of lunch. He had taken cocktails on to the terrace before lunch and had then gone up to remove his things from the  attic to another room. He had not looked out of the window during the morning and had seen nothing that could have any bearing upon the death of General Macarthur.
paraphrase: When Rogers was called to appear in court, he had practically no information to share. Throughout the entire morning, he had been occupied with various domestic chores and with getting lunch ready. He had carried cocktails out to the terrace just before lunch, and afterward, he had proceeded upstairs to relocate his belongings from the attic to a different room. He had not glanced out of the window at any point that morning and, consequently, had not observed anything pertinent to the demise of General Macarthur.

original: Grenouille’s case was nothing of the sort. There was not the least notion of God in his head. He was not doing penance nor waiting for some supernatural inspiration. He had withdrawn solely for his own personal pleasure, only to be near to himself. No longer distracted by anything external, he basked in his own existence and found it splendid. He lay in his stony crypt like his own corpse, hardly breathing, his heart hardly beating—and yet lived as intensively and dissolutely as ever a rake had lived in the wide world outside.
paraphrase: Grenouille's situation bore no resemblance to that. He harbored absolutely no concept of a divine being. He was neither undergoing spiritual self-punishment nor anticipating any form of transcendent enlightenment. His retreat was motivated purely by his individual enjoyment, specifically to be in close proximity to his own being. Having shed all external diversions, he luxuriated in his mere existence, perceiving it as magnificent. He reposed in his rock-hewn tomb as if he were his own deceased body, barely inhaling, his heart almost still—nevertheless, he experienced life with an intensity and abandon unmatched by any libertine in the expansive world beyond.

original: If she has given you one of her spells, you need not envy those of the fairies.' 'If it is strong enough to enchant your judgment, sir,' said Emily, 'while I disclose her images, I need NOT envy them. The lines go in a sort of tripping measure, which I thought might suit the subject well enough, but I fear they are too irregular.' THE GLOW-WORM How pleasant is the green-wood's deep-matted shade On a mid-summer's eve, when the fresh rain is o'er; When the yellow beams slope, and sparkle thro' the glade, And swiftly in the thin air the light swallows soar! But sweeter, sweeter still, when the sun sinks to rest, And twilight comes on, with the fairies so gay Tripping through the forest-walk, where flow'rs, unprest, Bow not their tall heads beneath their frolic play.
paraphrase:
"You have no cause to envy the spells of the fairies if she has bestowed one upon you." Emily responded, "Sir, if it possesses sufficient power to captivate your discernment, I assure you I have no need to envy them while I reveal her artistic creations. The verses flow in a rhythmic, somewhat playful cadence, which I believed would be quite fitting for the theme, though I worry they lack perfect uniformity."

THE GLOW-WORM

How delightful is the deeply intertwined canopy of the green-wood On a mid-summer's evening, after the recent rain has ceased; When the golden rays slant downwards, and shimmer through the open space, And gracefully through the sparse air the nimble swallows ascend! Yet even more enchanting, when the sun descends for its slumber, And dusk approaches, with the jubilant fairies Tripping through the forest path, where blossoms, untouched, Do not bend their elevated blooms beneath their lively games.

original: “A man who was influenced by both Kierkegaard and Nietzsche was the German existential philosopher Martin Heidegger. But we are going to concentrate on the French existentialist Jean-Paul Sartre, who lived from 1905 to 1980. He was the leading light among the existentialists—at least, to the broader public. His existentialism became especially popular in the forties, just after the war. Later on he allied himself with the Marxist movement in France, but he never became a member of any party.”
paraphrase: "The German existential philosopher Martin Heidegger drew inspiration from both Kierkegaard and Nietzsche. However, our focus will be on the French existentialist Jean-Paul Sartre, who was alive between the years 1905 and 1980. He stood out as the foremost figure among existentialists, particularly in the eyes of the general population. His brand of existentialism gained significant traction in the 1940s, immediately following the conclusion of the war. Subsequently, he formed an alliance with the Marxist movement in France, although he never formally joined any political party."

original: No shriekers and no screams.”   He shot her a look of pure poison and drew his dagger. Kat and Azra skirted the double spiral of the main stairwell and passed beneath the honeycomb archway of the vault approach. Storerooms filled wall to wall with impounded possessions passed away to either side. He provided the uniforms and the layout. Now he needs us far more than we need him.”   The muffled thud of a falling body whispered down the corridor, so quiet that Kat doubted she’d have noticed had she not been specifically listening out. Darravon indeed knew his business. The mooring house sentries were formality more than anything else, set to guard against the unlikely possibility of skelders scaling the temple’s outer walls. Likely they hadn’t even seen him coming. Scores of alcoves yawned wide, half hidden in the shadows beneath vaulted windows. A skeletal koilos stood within each, arms folded across its black-robed chest and yoke-like collar, gilded letters embossed into each naked skull. Scraps of broken chain hung from ceremonial bands about their wrists, representations of the shackles of avarice that had sealed their fate. Misers, debtors and cozeners all, their penance was to guard in death that which had ruled them in life. A gentle probe confirmed that her stolen glyphs held no sway over these koilos. If they woke, it would get very nasty very quickly. Did a koilos know anything of the person they’d been or the slaves they’d become? Would she, if her turn came? Even the thought of being trapped behind those empty eyes, screaming as Alabastra made a puppet of her body…   Better to settle the debt and never know the truth.
paraphrase: “Absolutely no yelling or screaming.” He gave her an intensely hostile glare and unsheathed his dagger. Kat and Azra navigated around the twin helix of the central staircase, then proceeded under the cellular archway leading to the vault entrance. Storage rooms, completely filled with confiscated items, extended on both sides. He supplied the attire and the architectural plans. Now, he relies on us significantly more than we depend on him.” A subdued impact, like a body collapsing, softly reverberated down the hallway, so faint that Kat questioned whether she would have detected it if she hadn’t been deliberately attentive. Darravon was undeniably proficient in his trade. The guards stationed at the mooring house were largely ceremonial, established to defend against the improbable event of skelders ascending the temple's external walls. It was probable they hadn't even perceived his arrival. Numerous alcoves stretched open, partially concealed within the dimness beneath arched windows. A gaunt koilos occupied each one, its arms crossed over its dark-colored, cloaked torso and yoked collar, with gold letters imprinted into every exposed skull. Remnants of fractured chains dangled from ritualistic bands around their wrists, symbolizing the bonds of greed that had determined their destiny. Misers, debtors, and swindlers alike, their posthumous atonement was to safeguard what had controlled them during their lifetime. A cautious investigation confirmed that her pilfered glyphs had no power over these koilos. If they awakened, the situation would rapidly become exceedingly unpleasant. Did a koilos retain any memory of the individual they once were or the enslaved existence they now endured? Would she, if her time came? The mere prospect of being imprisoned behind those vacant eyes, screaming as Alabastra manipulated her body like a marionette… It would be preferable to resolve the obligation and remain ignorant of the reality.

original: Gardiner readily promised every assistance in his power. -- Elizabeth, though expecting no less, thanked him with tears of gratitude; and all three being actuated by one spirit, every thing relating to their journey was speedily settled. They were to be off as soon as possible. ``But what is to be done about Pemberley?'' cried Mrs. Gardiner. ``John told us Mr. Darcy was here when you sent for us; -- was it so?'' ``Yes; and I told him we should not be able to keep our engagement. _That_ is all settled.'' ``That is all settled!'' repeated the other, as she ran into her room to prepare. ``And are they upon such terms as for her to disclose the real truth! Oh, that I knew how it was!'' But wishes were vain; or at best could serve only to amuse her in the hurry and confusion of the following hour. Had Elizabeth been at leisure to be idle, she would have remained certain that all employment was impossible to one so wretched as herself; but she had her share of business as well as her aunt, and amongst the rest there were notes to be written to all their friends in Lambton, with false excuses for their sudden departure. An hour, however, saw the whole completed; and Mr. Gardiner meanwhile having settled his account at the inn, nothing remained to be done but to go; and Elizabeth, after all the misery of the morning, found herself, in a shorter space of time than she could have supposed, seated in the carriage, and on the road to Longbourn.
paraphrase: Gardiner promptly pledged to provide all the help he could. Even though Elizabeth anticipated nothing less, she expressed her thanks with tears of appreciation; and since all three shared a common purpose, every detail concerning their trip was quickly finalized. Their departure was scheduled for the earliest possible moment. "But what about Pemberley?" exclaimed Mrs. Gardiner. "John informed us that Mr. Darcy was present when you called for us; was that accurate?" "Indeed; and I informed him that we would be unable to uphold our commitment. That matter is entirely resolved." "That matter is entirely resolved!" the other woman reiterated, as she hurried into her room to get ready. "And are they on such agreeable terms that she would reveal the unvarnished truth! Oh, if only I understood the situation!" However, such desires were futile; or at best, they merely served to distract her amidst the rush and disarray of the subsequent hour. If Elizabeth had had the luxury of idleness, she would have been convinced that any activity was beyond the capacity of someone as unhappy as she was; yet, she had her own tasks to attend to, much like her aunt, and among these were letters to be drafted for all their acquaintances in Lambton, containing fabricated justifications for their abrupt departure. Nevertheless, the entire process was finished within an hour; and Mr. Gardiner, in the interim, having paid his bill at the inn, left nothing further to be done except to leave; and Elizabeth, after enduring the morning's distress, discovered herself, in a surprisingly brief period, seated in the carriage, heading towards Longbourn.

original: In mid-October a young man was found dead down by the docks. There was no shortage of death among the poor that autumn, but this man’s murder seized the imagination of the entire city, for it had been achieved in such an ingenious way as to leave even the constabulary utterly baffled. The victim had been killed inside his shabby furnished room, the door locked from the inside. He had not merely been shot or stabbed, as one would expect from the waterfront and its denizens. No, the newspapers said he had been savaged, nearly torn to pieces, as though by a wild animal.

original: Then why was he sent to that place with them? But his father had told him that he would be no stranger there because his granduncle had presented an address to the liberator there fifty years before. You could know the people of that time by their old dress. It seemed to him a solemn time: and he wondered if that was the time when the fellows in Clongowes wore blue coats with brass buttons and yellow waistcoats and caps of rabbitskin and drank beer like grown-up people and kept greyhounds of their own to course the hares with.
paraphrase: So, what was the reason for his being dispatched to that location in their company? However, his father had assured him that he would not feel like an outsider there, as his great-uncle had delivered a speech to the liberator in that very place five decades prior. One could identify the inhabitants of that era by their antiquated attire. The period struck him as somber and significant; and he pondered whether that particular era was when the students at Clongowes donned blue coats adorned with brass buttons, sported yellow waistcoats, and wore caps fashioned from rabbitskin, consumed beer akin to adults, and owned their own greyhounds for hunting hares.

original: You're to call me George. Then I'll speak to you. But I shan't if you don't." "Oh!" said Anne, thinking that her new cousin was most extraordinary. "All right! I don't care what I call you. George is a nice name, I think. I don't much like Georgina. Anyway, you look like a boy." "Do I really?" said George, the frown leaving her face for a moment. "Mother was awfully cross with me when I cut my hair short. I had hair all round my neck; it was awful." The two girls stared at one another for a moment. "Don't you simply hate being a girl?" asked George. "No, of course not," said Anne. "You see- I do like pretty frocks- and I love my dolls- and you can't do that if you're a boy." "Pooh! Fancy bothering about pretty frocks," said George, in a scornful voice. "And dolls! Well, you are a baby, that's all I can say." Anne felt offended. "You're not very polite," she said. "You won't find that my brothers take much notice of you if you act as if you knew everything. They're real boys, not pretend boys, like you." "Well, if they're going to be nasty to me I shan't take any notice of them," said George, jumping out of bed. "I didn't want any of you to come, anyway. Interfering with my life here! I'm quite happy on my own. Now I've got to put up with a silly girl who likes frocks and dolls, and two stupid boy-cousins!" Anne felt that they had made a very bad beginning.

"My mother was exceedingly displeased when I opted for a short haircut. My hair used to be all around my neck; it was dreadful." The two young girls exchanged gazes for a brief interval. "You see— I genuinely enjoy attractive dresses— and I adore my dolls— and those are not activities one can engage in as a boy." "Nonsense! "You are not particularly courteous," she stated. "My brothers will not pay you much attention if you behave as though you possess all knowledge. They are genuine boys, not make-believe boys, like yourself." "Well, if they intend to be unpleasant towards me, I shall simply disregard them," George retorted, leaping from her bed. "I never desired any of you to come in the first place. Interfering with my existence here! I am perfectly content by myself. Now I am forced to tolerate a foolish girl who fancies dresses and dolls, and two idiotic boy-cousins!" Anne sensed that their initial encounter had been exceptionally poor.

original: He kept the lights off as he pulled the sheet covering Hap, tugging it down to his chest. It was the first thing hed fixed, knowing it was easier than a heart. The skin that had torn when Haps heart exploded had been too shredded to repair, the panel covering his power cavity broken. Hed replaced them with wood, knowing Hap would like it when he awoke. If he awoke. He climbed onto the table next to Hap, curling against him, making himself as small as he could. Hap was cold, skin like ice, but Vic didnt mind. He pulled the sheet back over them and lay his head on Haps shoulder.
paraphrase: With the lights off, he pulled back the sheet that lay over Hap, drawing it down to Hap's chest. This was the initial repair he had made, understanding that it was less complicated than fixing a heart. The skin, which had ripped when Hap's heart burst, was too mangled to be mended, and the panel protecting his power core was shattered. He had substituted these parts with wood, convinced that Hap would appreciate it upon regaining consciousness – assuming he would regain consciousness. He then ascended onto the table beside Hap, snuggling close and making himself as compact as possible. Hap felt frigid, his skin like ice, yet Vic was unconcerned. He drew the sheet back over both of them and rested his head on Hap's shoulder.

original: As a species, Bliddits were far from imposing. They stood merely a meter tall, with rounded, bulbous bodies, short legs, and thin arms. In contrast, Liquilids, his own kind, grew six times longer, boasting powerful muscles capable of crushing any prey. Not that they had resorted to such primitive methods of obtaining food in over a thousand years, but the strength remained ingrained in their biology. Their heads formed a triangular shape, with wide mouths filled with two rows of sawblade teeth, towering two meters above the ground. The remaining four meters extended behind them in a long, red-scaled tail, offering substantial protection against most weapons. Their wiry arms terminated in four fingers, each tipped with a sharp claw. Lu-puk emitted a hiss of amusement. Compared to the Liquilids, it seemed inconceivable that the Bliddits had survived evolution, ventured into space, and colonized hundreds of solar systems. Yet, they had built an empire. And they had proven to be worthy adversaries, cruel, even, when pushed into battle. They had gone beyond the norms of battle and captured hundredsno, thousandsof Liquilid warriors. Placing their prisoners in a deep hole, the Bliddits poured an acid-like compound on the captured warriors. Their screams could be heard from miles away. This vicious cycle of war lasted until the Liquilids discovered them, conquered them, and uncovered two intriguing revelations. First, the dozen jewel-like formations embedded in a Bliddits skin shimmered in rainbow hues when exposed to bright light, much like the one illuminating Lu-puks dinner table.
paraphrase: The Bliddits, as a species, were anything but intimidating. Their stature was a mere meter, characterized by spherical, bulging torsos, stubby legs, and slender arms. In stark contrast, his own species, the Liquilids, reached six times that length, possessing formidable musculature capable of pulverizing any victim. It had been over a millennium since they had resorted to such rudimentary hunting techniques, yet this inherent power persisted within their biological makeup. Their craniums were distinctly triangular, featuring expansive mouths armed with dual rows of serrated teeth, positioned two meters above the ground. The remaining four meters of their form trailed behind them as an elongated, crimson-scaled tail, offering considerable defense against the majority of armaments. Their wiry limbs culminated in four digits, each adorned with a keen claw. Lu-puk let out a soft, amused hiss. When juxtaposed with the Liquilids, it appeared utterly improbable that the Bliddits had not only endured the evolutionary process but had also embarked on interstellar travel and established colonies across hundreds of star systems. Nevertheless, they had constructed an empire. Furthermore, they had demonstrated themselves to be formidable opponents, even exhibiting cruelty when provoked into combat. They had transgressed the established conventions of warfare by seizing hundreds—no, thousands—of Liquilid combatants. After confining their captives within a deep chasm, the Bliddits then doused the imprisoned warriors with an acidic compound. The agonizing cries of these warriors resonated for miles. This brutal cycle of conflict endured until the Liquilids located them, subjugated them, and unearthed two fascinating discoveries. Firstly, the numerous jewel-like structures embedded in a Bliddit's epidermis radiated in iridescent colors when exposed to intense illumination, much like the light source illuminating Lu-puk's dining surface.

original: It also feels as though Id be abandoning a household in shambles if I left. While Dan and I are both working from home now, as a scientist he is considered essential personnel and expects hell need to go back in eventually, and its not easy for either of us to get a full days work done with schools closed. It gets harder still when remote learning begins in earnest; our kids are grieving, isolated, irritable, and bored, and the last thing they want is to be assigned hours of busywork on a Chromebook. Our younger daughters Individualized Education Programall the supports and accommodations we spent years advocating for and hammering out at stressful meetings with her school teamhas essentially disappeared overnight. At school, she has a one-to-one aide; at home, she has us: Dan takes the math block, I take language arts, and we split the other subjects. If one of us didnt sit with her for every lesson, shed be unable to manage the multiple online platforms or access her schoolwork at all. There are always dozens of tabs open on my phone: flights home, a smaller number every day; articles about virus transmissibility; articles analyzing (guessing at) the risks of flying; tables showing infection rates where I live, and in the cities I might have to fly through, and in my mothers county. One morning, while struggling to pay attention to my daughters reading-group Zoom lesson, I think about how responsibly attempting a solo trip at this moment would mean two weeks of quarantine at either end, in addition to however long I spent with my mom.

The situation becomes even more difficult once remote learning fully commences; our children are experiencing grief, isolation, irritability, and boredom, and the last thing they desire is to be burdened with hours of pointless assignments on a Chromebook. Our younger daughter's Individualized Education Program—all the accommodations and support systems we spent years advocating for and meticulously finalizing during challenging meetings with her school team—has practically vanished overnight. At school, she benefits from a dedicated one-on-one aide; at home, she relies on us: Dan handles the math segment, I manage language arts, and we divide the remaining subjects. If one of us weren't present with her for every lesson, she would be incapable of navigating the numerous online platforms or accessing her schoolwork whatsoever. My phone consistently has countless tabs open: flights back home, a number that decreases daily; pieces of writing concerning virus transmissibility; articles dissecting (or speculating about) the hazards of air travel; charts illustrating infection rates in my current location, in the cities I might need to transit through, and in my mother's county. One morning, while striving to concentrate on my daughter's reading-group Zoom lesson, I considered how undertaking a solo trip responsibly at this time would entail two weeks of quarantine at both ends of the journey, in addition to the duration I would spend with my mother.

original: That night? Immediately. He wanted to head west. Maybe to California. He said he heard people there were more tolerant. He said there was a chance we could be happy there. But I knew better. Archie shuffles to a stool. He sits, slumped, as if the sad memories hes recalling are literally weighing him down. Running away is never as easy as it seems. I know because Id already done it, fleeing a family who hated me because I was different, because I wasnt like most other boys. But I found my way hereand to Virginia. Did she know? I say. Archie responds with a nod. Its one of the reasons I loved her so much. She didnt judge me. Or shame me. Or, thank goodness, try to change who I am. She simply accepted me. And I couldnt leave her. Not when she was pregnant. Not when she needed me. Because thats another thing Ricardo told methat Berniece had spotted her the night before and knew of her condition. That meant everyone would soon know. When that happened, Virginia would need me more than ever. So you stayed, I say, meaning not just that night but all the ones after it. Decades of nights in which hed sneak into Virginias room to check on her and wish her pleasant dreams. I stayed, Archie says. Ricardo left. I never heard from him again. The sadness of his story leaves me convinced that Hopes End is cursed in some way.

He also suggested there was a possibility we could find happiness there. However, I possessed a clearer understanding. Archie laboriously moves towards a stool. He settles onto it, his posture slouched, as if the sorrowful recollections he is bringing forth are physically burdening him. Escaping one's circumstances is never as straightforward as it appears. I am aware of this because I had already experienced it, having escaped a family who harbored animosity towards me due to my unique qualities, because I did not conform to the typical mold of other boys. Nevertheless, I discovered my path to this very place—and to Virginia. Was she aware? I inquire. Archie acknowledges with a nod. "That was one of the many reasons I held such deep affection for her. She refrained from criticizing me. Or bringing shame upon me. Or, thankfully, attempting to alter my true self. She simply embraced me for who I am. And I found myself unable to depart from her. Because there was another piece of information Ricardo imparted to me—that Berniece had seen her the preceding night and was aware of her pregnancy. original: When his father disappeared, his mother did not show any surprise or any violent grief, but a sudden change came over her. She seemed to have become completely spiritless. It was evident even to Winston that she was waiting for something that she knew must happen. She did everything that was needed--cooked, washed, mended, made the bed, swept the floor, dusted the mantelpiece--always very slowly and with a curious lack of superfluous motion, like an artist's lay-figure moving of its own accord.
paraphrase: Upon the disappearance of her husband, his mother displayed neither astonishment nor intense sorrow, yet a profound alteration occurred within her. She appeared to have been entirely divested of her spirit. It was apparent, even to Winston, that she was anticipating an event she was certain would transpire. She performed all necessary tasks—preparing meals, laundering clothes, repairing items, making the bed, sweeping the floor, and dusting the mantelpiece—consistently at a deliberate pace and with an unusual absence of unnecessary movement, akin to an artist's mannequin moving autonomously.

original: The four copper horses had been cast in the fourth century by an unknown Greek sculptor on the island of Chios, where they remained until Theodosius II whisked them off to Constantinople for display at the Hippodrome. Then, during the Fourth Crusade, when Venetian forces sacked Constantinople, the ruling doge demanded the four precious statues be transported via ship all the way back to Venice, a nearly impossible feat because of their size and weight. The horses arrived in Venice in 1254, and were installed in front of the facade of St. Mark’s Cathedral.

Langdon wondered how a handicapped man could possibly have overpowered the camerlengo.

"Certain ideals are worth sacrificing one's life for," the Hassassin had declared. Langdon pondered how a man with a disability could have possibly subdued the camerlengo.

original: "Could you have seen him charge the monster as a bull might charge a grizzly--absolutely without sign of fear or hesitation--you would have believed him more than human. "Could you have seen those mighty muscles knotting under the brown skin--could you have seen them force back those awful fangs--you too would have thought him invincible. "And could you have seen the chivalrous treatment which he accorded a strange girl of a strange race, you would feel the same absolute confidence in him that I feel." "You have won your suit, my fair pleader," cried the captain. "This court finds the defendant not guilty, and the cruiser shall wait a few days longer that he may have an opportunity to come and thank the divine Portia." "For the Lord's sake honey," cried Esmeralda. "You all don't mean to tell ME that you're going to stay right here in this here land of carnivable animals when you all got the opportunity to escapade on that boat? Don't you tell me THAT, honey." "Why, Esmeralda! You should be ashamed of yourself," cried Jane. "Is this any way to show your gratitude to the man who saved your life twice?" "Well, Miss Jane, that's all jest as you say; but that there forest man never did save us to stay here. He done save us so we all could get AWAY from here. I expect he be mighty peevish when he find we ain't got no more sense than to stay right here after he done give us the chance to get away.
paraphrase: "Had you witnessed his assault on the creature, much like a bull confronting a grizzly—utterly devoid of any indication of fright or reluctance—you would have been convinced of his superhuman nature. "If you had observed the powerful muscles bunching beneath his tanned skin—if you had seen them repel those terrifying fangs—you, too, would have perceived him as unconquerable. "And had you witnessed the honorable way he treated an unfamiliar woman from an alien people, you would share the complete trust in him that I possess." "Your case is successful, my eloquent advocate," the captain exclaimed. "This tribunal declares the accused innocent, and the vessel will remain for a few more days, allowing him the chance to express his gratitude to the magnificent Portia." "For heaven's sake, dearest," Esmeralda shrieked. "You surely aren't suggesting to ME that you intend to remain in this place populated by predatory creatures when you have the chance to flee on that ship? Don't even think of telling me THAT, dearest." "Esmeralda, really! You ought to be mortified," Jane retorted. "Is this how you demonstrate your appreciation to the individual who rescued you on two separate occasions?" "Well, Miss Jane, that's precisely as you articulate it; however, that man from the wilderness never rescued us with the intention of us remaining here. He saved us so that we could all depart from this location. I anticipate he will be exceptionally displeased when he discovers we lack the good judgment to stay right here after he provided us with the means to escape.

original: But she screwed herself up to the work in hand, since she could not get out of it, and answered-- "I came to see your mother, sir." "I am afraid you cannot see her--she is an invalid," replied thepresent representative of the spurious house; for this was Mr Alec, the only son of the lately deceased gentleman. "Cannot I answer your purpose? What is the business you wish to see her about?" "It isn't business--it is--I can hardly say what!" "Pleasure?" "Oh no. Why, sir, if I tell you, it will seem---" Tess's sense of a certain ludicrousness in her errand was now so strong that, notwithstanding her awe of him, and her general discomfort at being here, her rosy lips curved towards a smile, much to the attraction of the swarthy Alexander. "It is so very foolish," she stammered; "I fear can't tell you!" "Never mind; I like foolish things. Try again, my dear," said he kindly. "Mother asked me to come," Tess continued; "and, indeed, I was in the mind to do so myself likewise. But I did not think it would be like this. I came, sir, to tell you that we are of the same family as you." "Ho! Poor relations?" "Yes." "Stokes?" "No; d'Urbervilles." "Ay, ay; I mean d'Urbervilles." "Our names are worn away to Durbeyfield; but we have several proofs that we are d'Urbervilles. Antiquarians hold we are,--and--and we have an old seal, marked with a ramping lion on a shield, and a castle over him.
paraphrase: However, she compelled herself to focus on the immediate task, given that escape was not an option, and responded, "I arrived to visit your mother, sir." "I regret to inform you that you cannot see her – she is unwell," stated the current occupant of the fabricated estate; for this individual was Mr. Alec, the sole heir of the recently deceased gentleman. "Can I not fulfill your objective? What is the matter you wish to discuss with her?" "It isn't a professional matter – it is – I struggle to articulate it!" "Is it for enjoyment?" "Oh, certainly not. Sir, if I disclose it, it will appear..." Tess's perception of the somewhat absurd nature of her mission was now so pronounced that, despite her reverence for him and her overall uneasiness in this setting, her full, pink lips began to curve into a faint smile, which greatly captivated the dark-complexioned Alexander. "It is truly quite silly," she stammered; "I am afraid I cannot tell you!" "Never mind; I appreciate silly things. Please try once more, my dear," he uttered with kindness. "Mother requested my presence," Tess proceeded; "and, truthfully, I was inclined to do so myself as well. However, I did not anticipate it would unfold in this manner. I came, sir, to inform you that we share a familial connection with you." "Indeed! Are you less fortunate relatives?" "Yes." "Stokes?" "No; d'Urbervilles." "Ah, yes; I understand, d'Urbervilles." "Our surname has been altered to Durbeyfield through time; but we possess multiple pieces of evidence confirming our d'Urberville lineage. Historians assert our connection, and – and we possess an ancient seal, imprinted with a rampant lion on a shield, surmounted by a castle."

Detective Nat FeltonLiz calls him Nathaniel every single timelikes a wet shave, a spearmint toothpaste and dark-coloured towels, and when I get out of the bathroom and Im thinking I might just have a quick look along the hall, his wife is holding a gun on me, dead steady and not amused at all. Dont feel bad, she says, you did it really well. She waggles the gun and I sit back down at the dining table. What gave me away? Nothing. Im a cops wife. I smelled you. Cal Sounder. She acts like she doesnt know the name. Maybe she doesnt. She doesnt talk to me and she also doesnt sit, or stop looking at me, for an hour. She just holds the gun level at my face until Felton gets home and calls me a fucking asshole. What the fuck are you doing in my house? I guess I nearly trust you. You nearly trust me so you sneak into my house and go through my shave bag? Yeah. Well that is a messed-up way of being in the world you have there, Sounder, so why dont you fuck off? And actually when you get there you can just keep on fucking. How would that be? Im sorry, man. The fuck is wrong with you? A guy with a scratch on his left boot drove a car over my witness and put a bullet in me.
paraphrase: Upon entering the private area, I surveyed my surroundings. Detective Nat Felton – Liz, his wife, invariably refers to him as Nathaniel – has a preference for wet shaves, spearmint-flavored toothpaste, and towels that are dark in hue. As I emerged from the bathroom, contemplating a brief inspection of the hallway, his wife confronted me, brandishing a firearm with unwavering steadiness and a complete lack of amusement. “Don’t feel bad,” she stated, “you executed it quite skillfully.” She gestured with the weapon, prompting me to reseat myself at the dining table. “What betrayed my presence?” I inquired. “Nothing,” she replied. “I’m a police officer’s wife. I detected your presence. Cal Sounder.” She feigned unfamiliarity with the name, though it’s possible she genuinely didn't recognize it. For the next hour, she neither spoke to me nor sat down, nor did she cease her vigilant observation, maintaining the gun pointed directly at my face until Felton’s arrival. He greeted me with, “You fucking asshole. What in the hell are you doing inside my residence? I almost put my trust in you.” “You almost put your trust in me, so you infiltrate my home and rummage through my shaving kit?” I retorted. “Indeed,” he responded. “That is a truly warped approach to existence that you possess, Sounder, so why don’t you just depart? And in fact, once you arrive wherever you’re going, you can simply continue your destructive behavior. How does that sound?” “My apologies, friend,” I offered. “What in the world is wrong with you?” he demanded. “A man with a scuff mark on his left boot drove a vehicle over my witness and then shot me.”

original: Fortunately, the proposal died. Next time we wont be so lucky, a person familiar with the discussions told me, envisioning a Trump-like future president. Well have a military run by mercenaries. A privatized force. Weaponized for political purposes. Policing U.S. city streets. If thats how the shield and the sword of government are recast, then Tom Warricks caricature of the Next Trump commanding his own forces doesnt seem so hyperbolic. A junior gestapo, as he put it, is exactly what it would be. * * * When I reflect on the nightmare scenarioof an American president hijacking the military for nefarious endsI like to believe there are safety valves. Thats the type of moment when the Twenty-Fifth Amendment gets invoked, isnt it? Surely the presidents cabinet would save the day by ejecting him from office if he tried to turn the armed forces against the American people. But I know better. The Next Trumps cabinet will be stacked with loyalists. If they think about flipping, theyll be watched. Top officials are routinely tracked so they can be whisked away in the event of a crisis. A paranoid president would use those same security measures as a trip wire to determine whether his cabinet was conveningand conspiringagainst him. We can protect our institutions up to a point with obvious remedies. Congress can curtail the two-hundred-year-old law that allows a president to deploy the military on U.S. soil, and legislators should make it harder for the White House to misuse the armed forces.
paraphrase: The suggestion, thankfully, was unsuccessful. "Next time, our fortune won't be as great," a source familiar with the conversations confided, imagining a future president akin to Trump. "Our military will be controlled by hired soldiers. A private army. Utilized as a weapon for political objectives. Patrolling the urban areas of the U.S." If the government's protective and offensive capabilities are reconfigured in such a manner, then Tom Warrick's satirical depiction of a future Trump commanding his personal forces no longer appears exaggerated. "A junior gestapo," as he described it, would be precisely accurate. * * * When I contemplate the horrific scenario of an American president misusing the military for sinister objectives, I prefer to believe that safeguards exist. "That's precisely when the Twenty-Fifth Amendment would be invoked, wouldn't it?" Certainly, the president's cabinet would intervene decisively by removing him from power if he attempted to direct the armed forces against the populace of America. However, I am more informed. The cabinet of a future Trump will be filled with devoted supporters. Should they consider rebellion, they will be under surveillance. High-ranking officials are regularly monitored so they can be swiftly moved in a time of emergency. A distrustful president would employ these identical security protocols as an early warning system to ascertain if his cabinet was assembling—and plotting—against him. We can safeguard our governmental structures to a certain extent with evident solutions. Congress possesses the authority to restrict the two-century-old statute that permits a president to deploy the military within U.S. territory, and lawmakers ought to make it more challenging for the White House to improperly utilize the armed forces.

original: I waited in the hall until I had composed myself. Reminded myself I must act naturally. Then I found Madge in her room, sitting at her dressing table, brushing out her wavy blond hair before a mirror. She was in the same pretty white dress she'd worn on reaping day. She saw my reflection behind her and smiled. “Look at you. Like you came right off the streets of the Capitol.”
paraphrase: I remained in the corridor until I had regained my composure. I made sure to recall that I needed to behave as if everything was normal. Subsequently, I located Madge within her bedroom, where she was seated at her vanity, meticulously brushing her undulating blonde hair while looking into a mirror. She was attired in the identical attractive white gown she had sported on the day of the reaping. Catching sight of my image in the mirror behind her, she offered a smile. "Just look at you. It's as if you stepped directly out of the Capitol's thoroughfares."

original: Had the Cossacks pursued the French, without heeding what was behind and around them, they would have captured Murat and everything there. That was what the officers desired. But it was impossible to make the Cossacks budge when once they had got booty and prisoners. None of them listened to orders. Fifteen hundred prisoners and thirty-eight guns were taken on the spot, besides standards and (what seemed most important to the Cossacks) horses, saddles, horsecloths, and the like. All this had to be dealt with, the prisoners and guns secured, the booty dividednot without some shouting and even a little themselvesand it was on this that the Cossacks all busied themselves.
paraphrase: If the Cossacks had chased the French troops, disregarding their surroundings and what lay behind them, they would have seized Murat and all his possessions. This was the wish of the commanding officers. However, it proved impossible to motivate the Cossacks to move forward once they had acquired plunder and captives. Not a single one of them paid attention to commands. On that very location, 1,500 prisoners and 38 cannons were secured, along with regimental flags and, what the Cossacks considered most valuable, steeds, riding equipment, horse blankets, and similar items. All these acquisitions required processing; the captives and artillery needed to be secured, and the spoils distributed—a process that involved a fair amount of yelling and even some minor altercations among themselves. The Cossacks were completely engrossed in these tasks.

It was a fine grey-green tweed, very elegant with a nipped-in waist and slanted pocket flaps. Lucy had put leather patches on the elbow to preserve it; you couldn't buy clothes like this anymore. She took it down to the living room, where Henry was putting his boots on.

original: When he saw me, he gasped out, "Then it was you, and just arrived. How is she? Are we too late? Did you not get my telegram?" I answered as quickly and coherently as I could that I had only got his telegram early in the morning, and had not a minute in coming here, and that I could not make any one in the house hear me. He paused and raised his hat as he said solemnly, "Then I fear we are too late. God's will be done!" With his usual recuperative energy, he went on, "Come. If there be no way open to get in, we must make one. Time is all in all to us now." We went round to the back of the house, where there was a kitchen window. The Professor took a small surgical saw from his case, and handing it to me, pointed to the iron bars which guarded the window. I attacked them at once and had very soon cut through three of them. Then with a long, thin knife we pushed back the fastening of the sashes and opened the window. I helped the Professor in, and followed him. There was no one in the kitchen or in the servants' rooms, which were close at hand. We tried all the rooms as we went along, and in the dining room, dimly lit by rays of light through the shutters, found four servant women lying on the floor.
paraphrase: Upon seeing me, he exclaimed with a gasp, "So it was you, and you've just arrived. How is she doing? Have we come too late? Did my telegram not reach you?" I responded as swiftly and clearly as possible, stating that I had only received his telegram that morning, and had not wasted a moment in getting there, and that I had been unable to get anyone in the house to respond to me. He paused, then lifted his hat and declared gravely, "Then I am afraid we are too late. May God's will be done!" Displaying his characteristic resilience, he continued, "Come. If there's no existing entry, we must create one. Time is of the essence for us now." We proceeded to the rear of the dwelling, where a kitchen window was located. The Professor extracted a small surgical saw from his kit, handed it to me, and gestured towards the iron bars protecting the window. I immediately set to work on them and quickly managed to cut through three. Following that, using a long, slender knife, we manipulated the sash fastening and opened the window. I assisted the Professor in entering, and then followed him inside. The kitchen was empty, as were the nearby servants' quarters. We systematically checked every room as we progressed, and in the dining room, which was faintly illuminated by light filtering through the shutters, we discovered four servant women prostrate on the floor.

original: As he turned to follow Speedwell up the run that led into the wood, he found Clover beside him. ‘You and your friends must find it strange to go outside and eat grass,’ he said. ‘You’ll get used to it, you know. And I can promise you that Hazel-rah was right when he told you it’s a better life here than in a hutch. Come with me and I’ll show you a patch of nice, short tail-grass, if Bigwig hasn’t had it all while I’ve been away.’
paraphrase: As he pivoted to accompany Speedwell along the path leading into the forest, he noticed Clover at his side. He remarked, "It must seem peculiar to you and your companions, venturing outdoors to consume grass." He continued, "You will adapt to it, rest assured. And I can assure you that Hazel-rah's assertion was accurate when he informed you that existence here surpasses that of a hutch. Join me, and I will guide you to a section of pleasant, low-lying tail-grass, assuming Bigwig hasn't already devoured it entirely during my absence."
