original: At five in  the morning it was still quite dark. The troops of the center, the reserves, and Bagration’s right flank had not yet moved, but on the left flank the columns of infantry, cavalry, and artillery, which were to be the first to descend the heights to attack the French right flank and drive it into the Bohemian mountains according to plan, were already up and astir. The smoke of the campfires, into which they were throwing everything superfluous, made the eyes smart. It was cold and dark. The officers were hurriedly drinking tea and breakfasting, the soldiers, munching biscuit and beating a tattoo with their feet to warm themselves, gathering round the fires throwing into the flames the remains of sheds, chairs, tables, wheels, tubs, and everything that they did not want or could not carry away with them. Austrian column guides were moving in and out among the Russian troops and served as heralds of the advance. As soon as an Austrian officer showed himself near a commanding officer’s quarters, the regiment began to move: the soldiers ran from the fires, thrust their pipes into their boots, their bags into the carts, got their muskets ready, and formed rank. The officers buttoned up their coats, buckled on their swords and pouches, and moved along the ranks shouting. The train drivers and orderlies harnessed and packed the wagons and tied on the loads. The adjutants and battalion and regimental commanders mounted, crossed themselves, gave final instructions, orders, and commissions to the baggage men who remained behind, and the monotonous tramp of thousands of feet resounded. The column moved forward without knowing where and unable, from the masses around them, the smoke and the increasing fog, to see either the place they were leaving or that to which they were going.
paraphrase: At 5 AM, profound darkness still enveloped the surroundings. The central forces, the reserves, and Bagration's right wing remained stationary. However, on the left flank, the infantry, cavalry, and artillery columns designated to initiate the descent from the elevated ground to engage the French right flank and, as per strategy, push them into the Bohemian mountains, were already awake and active. The acrid smoke emanating from the campfires, into which all non-essential items were being discarded, caused a stinging sensation in the eyes. The air was chilly and visibility low. Officers hastily consumed tea and their morning meal, while soldiers, chewing on hardtack and tapping their feet rhythmically to generate warmth, congregated around the fires. They were tossing into the flames discarded shed remnants, chairs, tables, wheels, barrels, and any other items they deemed unnecessary or too cumbersome to transport. Austrian column guides were circulating among the Russian troops, acting as harbingers of the impending advance. The appearance of an Austrian officer near a commanding officer’s quarters signaled the regiment to commence movement: soldiers swiftly abandoned their fires, tucked their pipes into their boots, stowed their bags onto carts, prepared their muskets, and assembled into formation. Officers buttoned their coats, secured their swords and ammunition pouches, and proceeded along the ranks, issuing commands. Drivers of supply wagons and orderlies yoked animals, packed the wagons, and fastened their loads. Adjutants and commanders of battalions and regiments mounted their horses, made the sign of the cross, delivered last-minute instructions, orders, and assignments to the baggage handlers who were staying behind, and then the rhythmic thud of countless footsteps filled the air. The column began its forward progression, unaware of its destination and unable, due to the dense surrounding troops, the smoke, and the intensifying fog, to discern either the location they were departing from or the one they were approaching.

original: "All right, Stil," Paul said. "We'll go back." But he took one final look around through the telescope -- studying the plain with its tall ships, the gleaming metal hutment, the silent city, the frigates of the Harkonnen mercenaries. Then he slid backward around a scarp of rock. His place at the telescope was taken by a Fedaykin guardsman. Paul emerged into a shallow depression in the Shield Wall's surface. It was a place about thirty meters in diameter and some three meters deep, a natural feature of the rock that the Fremen had hidden beneath a translucent camouflage cover. Communications equipment was clustered around a hole in the wall to the right. Fedaykin guards deployed through the depression waited for Muad'Dib's command to attack. Two men emerged from the hole by the communications equipment, spoke to the guards there. Paul glanced at Stilgar, nodded in the direction of the two men. "Get their report, Stil." Stilgar moved to obey. Paul crouched with his back to the rock, stretching his muscles, straightened. He saw Stilgar sending the two men back into that dark hole in the rock, thought about the long climb down that narrow man-made tunnel to the floor of the basin. Stilgar crossed to Paul. "What was so important that they couldn't send a cielago with the message?" Paul asked. "They're saving their birds for the battle," Stilgar said. He glanced at the communications equipment, back to Paul.
paraphrase: "Very well, Stil," Paul stated. "We shall return." However, he cast one last gaze across the landscape via the telescope, examining the expanse with its prominent vessels, the shimmering metallic settlement, the hushed urban area, and the frigates belonging to the Harkonnen hired soldiers. Following this, he retreated behind a rocky incline. A Fedaykin guard then occupied his position at the telescope. Paul surfaced into a shallow indentation on the Shield Wall's exterior. This area measured approximately thirty meters in width and about three meters in depth, a naturally occurring geological formation that the Fremen had concealed with a transparent camouflage covering. Telecommunications apparatus was grouped around an aperture in the wall on the right side. Fedaykin guards, strategically positioned throughout the depression, awaited Muad'Dib's order to launch an offensive. Two individuals appeared from the opening near the communications gear and conversed with the guards stationed there. Paul briefly looked at Stilgar, then gestured with his head towards the two men. "Obtain their update, Stil." Stilgar promptly moved to comply. Paul hunkered down with his back against the rock, flexing his limbs, and then stood upright. He observed Stilgar dispatching the two men back into that dim cavity in the rock, contemplating the arduous descent through that restricted, artificial passage leading to the basin's floor. Stilgar then approached Paul. "What information was so critical that they couldn't dispatch a cielago with the message?" Paul inquired. "They are preserving their avian messengers for the impending conflict," Stilgar replied. He directed his gaze at the communications equipment, then back to Paul.

original: Ned did not like to think on that. "Here," he told his king. Robert nodded silently, knelt, and bowed his head. There were three tombs, side by side. Lord Rickard Stark, Ned's father, had a long, stern face. The stonemason had known him well. He sat with quiet dignity, stone fingers holding tight to the sword across his lap, but in life all swords had failed him. In two smaller sepulchres on either side were his children. Brandon had been twenty when he died, strangled by order of the Mad King Aerys Targaryen only a few short days before he was to wed Catelyn Tully of Riverrun.
paraphrase: Ned preferred not to dwell on that thought. "Over here," he directed his king. Robert gave a silent nod, then lowered himself to his knees and bowed his head. There were three burial places, arranged next to each other. Lord Rickard Stark, Ned's father, possessed a long, severe countenance. The stonemason had clearly known him intimately. He was depicted seated with serene dignity, his stone fingers gripping the sword resting across his lap, yet during his lifetime, all weapons had proven useless to him. In two smaller burial chambers flanking him were his offspring. Brandon had reached the age of twenty at the time of his death, having been choked to death on the command of the Mad King Aerys Targaryen merely a few days prior to his scheduled marriage to Catelyn Tully from Riverrun.

original: "Shut your eyes," said Mary, drawing her footstool closer, "and I will do what my Ayah used to do in India. I will pat your hand and stroke it and sing something quite low." "I should like that perhaps," he said drowsily. Somehow she was sorry for him and did not want him to lie awake, so she leaned against the bed and began to stroke and pat his hand and sing a very low little chanting song in Hindustani. "That is nice," he said more drowsily still, and she went on chanting and stroking, but when she looked at him again his black lashes were lying close against his cheeks, for his eyes were shut and he was fast asleep.
paraphrase: "Close your eyes," Mary instructed, pulling her footstool nearer. "I'll do for you what my Ayah in India always did. I'll gently pat and stroke your hand, and softly sing a quiet tune." "Perhaps I would enjoy that," he murmured, his voice heavy with sleep. Feeling a pang of sympathy for him and wishing to spare him from lying awake, she rested against the bed. She then commenced to stroke and pat his hand, and hum a soft, rhythmic chant in Hindustani. "That feels pleasant," he uttered, even more deeply entranced by sleep. She continued her gentle chanting and stroking, but when she glanced at him once more, his dark eyelashes lay peacefully against his cheeks; his eyes were closed, and he had drifted into a sound sleep.

original: Paneloux stopped, and at that moment Rieux could hear, through the doors, the wind picking up as its howls seemed to double outside. At this very instant, the father said that the virtue of total acceptance he spoke of could not be understood in the narrow sense he usually gave it, for it was neither about banal resignation, nor even difficult humility. It was about humiliation, but humiliation where the humiliated person was consenting. Of course, the suffering of a child was humiliating for the mind and for the heart. But that was why he had to go into it. But that was why, and Paneloux assured his audience that what he was about to say was not easy, they had to want it because God wanted it. This was the only way the Christian would spare himself nothing and, with no way out, get straight to the essential choice. He would choose to believe everything so as not to be reduced to denying everything. And like the brave women in the churches these days who, having learned that the buboes formed as the body’s natural way of rejecting its infection, would say: “My God, give him buboes,” the Christian would know how to abandon himself to divine will, even if it was perplexing. You could not say: “This I understand, but that is unacceptable,” you had to jump into the heart of the unacceptable that was offered to us, precisely so that we could make our choice. The suffering of children was our bitter bread, but without this bread, our souls would perish from spiritual hunger.
paraphrase: Paneloux paused, and just then, Rieux could detect the escalating wind through the doors, its cries seemingly intensifying outside. At that precise moment, the priest clarified that the complete acceptance he had mentioned should not be interpreted in the confined manner typically associated with it, as it was neither mere resignation nor even a challenging form of humility. Instead, it concerned humiliation, specifically a humiliation willingly embraced by the individual experiencing it. Naturally, the suffering endured by a child was intellectually and emotionally demeaning. However, that was precisely why one had to confront it. Furthermore, Paneloux reassured those listening that his next statement would not be easily accepted: they needed to desire this humiliation because it was God's will. This was the sole method for a Christian to hold nothing back and, without any alternative, arrive directly at the fundamental decision. They would opt to embrace every belief rather than be forced into rejecting all of them. And similar to the courageous women in the churches during those times who, upon learning that buboes represented the body's natural defense against infection, would exclaim: "My God, give him buboes," a Christian would understand how to surrender to divine purpose, regardless of its bewildering nature. One could not declare: "I grasp this, but that is intolerable." Instead, one had to plunge wholeheartedly into the very core of the unacceptable presented to us, specifically to empower us to make our definitive choice. The anguish of children constituted our harsh sustenance, yet without this sustenance, our spirits would wither from spiritual starvation.

original: The funny thing is that you feel terrified to stay and I feel terrified to go. Foxes here, weasels there, Fiver in the middle, begone dull care!’   He pulled out a burnet leaf and ate it slowly, concealing his fear as best he could; for all his instincts were warning him of the dangers in the unknown country beyond the warren. So between now and the time when we go, we ought to persuade as many as we can to join us.’   ‘I think there are one or two in the Owsla who might be worth sounding,’ said Bigwig. But they won’t come because of Fiver. They’ll be juniors, discontented fellows like me. You need to have heard Fiver yourself to be convinced by him. He’s convinced me. It’s obvious that he’s been sent some kind of message, and I believe in these things. I can’t think why he didn’t convince the Threarah.’   ‘Because the Threarah doesn’t like anything he hasn’t thought of for himself,’ answered Hazel. We’ve got to try to collect some more rabbits and meet again here,  fu Inlé. The danger’s coming closer all the time – whatever it is – and besides, the Threarah isn’t going to like it if he finds out that you’ve been  trying to get at rabbits in the Owsla, Bigwig. Neither is Captain Holly, I dare say. They won’t mind odds-and-ends like us clearing off, but they won’t want to lose you. If I were in your place, I’d be careful whom I picked to talk to.’   .
paraphrase:
It's quite ironic how you're filled with dread at the prospect of remaining, while I'm gripped by terror at the thought of leaving. "Foxes here, weasels there, Fiver in the middle, begone dull care!" He deliberately plucked a burnet leaf and consumed it with measured slowness, meticulously masking his apprehension; every innate sense within him was vehemently signaling the perilous nature of the uncharted territory situated beyond the familiar warren. Consequently, between this very moment and our eventual departure, our objective should be to sway as many individuals as possible to align with our cause.

Bigwig remarked, "I suspect there might be a couple of individuals within the Owsla who warrant investigation." However, their motivation for joining won't stem from Fiver's influence. They'll likely be younger, dissatisfied individuals, much like myself. One truly needs to have personally encountered Fiver to be persuaded by his convictions. He has certainly convinced me. It's plainly evident that he has received some form of communication, and I place my trust in such occurrences. I'm at a loss as to why he failed to sway the Threarah.

Hazel responded, "Because the Threarah dismisses anything that didn't originate from his own thoughts." We must endeavor to gather a greater number of rabbits and reconvene at this precise location, after moonrise. The peril is steadily encroaching – whatever its precise nature – and furthermore, the Threarah will not be pleased if he discovers your attempts to recruit rabbits from the Owsla, Bigwig. Captain Holly, I presume, will share a similar sentiment. They won't object to insignificant individuals like us departing, but they will undoubtedly resist losing you. If I were in your position, I would exercise caution in selecting those with whom I chose to converse.

original: Ajofia laughed in his guttural voice. It was like the laugh of rusty metal. "They are strangers," he said, "and they are ignorant. But let that pass." He turned round to his comrades and saluted them, calling them the fathers of Umuofia. He dug his rattling spear into the ground and it shook with metallic life. Then he turned once more to the missionary and his interpreter.
paraphrase: Ajofia chuckled deeply, emitting a rough, throaty sound that resembled the grating of corroded metal. "These individuals are outsiders," he declared, "and they lack understanding. However, let us overlook that." He then pivoted to face his fellow men, greeting them and bestowing upon them the title of Umuofia's patriarchs. He thrust his shaking spear into the earth, causing it to vibrate with a metallic vitality. Following this, he redirected his attention to the missionary and his translator.

original: Ralph could no longer ignore his speech. The blood was hot in his cheeks. "You haven't got the conch," he said. "Sit down." Jack's face went so white that the freckles showed as clear, brown flecks. He licked his lips and remained standing. "This is a hunter's job." The rest of the boys watched intently. Piggy, finding himself uncomfortably embroiled, slid the conch to Ralph's knees and sat down. The silence grew oppressive and Piggy held his breath. "This is more than a hunter's job," said Ralph at last, "because you can't track the beast. And don't you want to be rescued?" He turned to the assembly. "Don't you all want to be rescued?" He looked back at Jack. "I said before, the fire is the main thing. Now the fire must be out--" The old exasperation saved him and gave him the energy to attack. "Hasn't anyone got any sense? We've got to relight that fire. You never thought of that, Jack, did you? Or don't any of you want to be rescued?" Yes, they wanted to be rescued, there was no doubt about that; and with a violent swing to Ralph's side, the crisis passed. Piggy let out his breath with a gasp, reached for it again and failed. He lay against a log, his mouth gaping, blue shadows creeping round his lips. Nobody minded him. "Now think, Jack. Is there anywhere on the island you haven't been?" Unwillingly Jack answered.
paraphrase: Ralph found it impossible to disregard what he was saying. A flush of heat spread across his cheeks. "You do not possess the conch," he stated. "Please take a seat." Jack's complexion became so pale that his freckles stood out distinctly as brown specks. He moistened his lips but did not sit down. "This task falls to a hunter." The other boys observed with keen interest. Piggy, feeling uncomfortably caught in the middle, pushed the conch towards Ralph's knees and took his seat. The quiet became stifling, and Piggy held his breath. "This involves more than simply a hunter's duty," Ralph finally declared, "since the beast cannot be tracked by you. And do you not desire to be saved?" He directed his gaze at the gathering. "Do all of you not wish to be rescued?" He then looked back at Jack. "As I mentioned previously, the fire holds primary importance. Now, the fire must be extinguished--" His long-standing frustration rescued him, providing the impetus to confront the situation. "Does no one here possess any common sense? We absolutely must rekindle that fire. You never even considered that, Jack, did you? Or is it that none of you actually want to be rescued?" Indeed, they did wish to be rescued, of that there was no question; and with an abrupt shift of allegiance to Ralph's position, the critical moment subsided. Piggy exhaled with a gasp, attempted to inhale again but failed. He rested against a fallen tree, his mouth agape, with faint blue discoloration emerging around his lips. No one paid him any attention. "Now, consider this, Jack. Is there any part of the island that you have not explored?" Jack responded reluctantly.

original: How could any vanquish such a mighty antagonist? Like two charging bulls they came together, and like two wolves sought each other's throat. Against the long canines of the ape was pitted the thin blade of the man's knife. Jane--her lithe, young form flattened against the trunk of a great tree, her hands tight pressed against her rising and falling bosom, and her eyes wide with mingled horror, fascination, fear, and admiration--watched the primordial ape battle with the primeval man for possession of a woman--for her. As the great muscles of the man's back and shoulders knotted beneath the tension of his efforts, and the huge biceps and forearm held at bay those mighty tusks, the veil of centuries of civilization and culture was swept from the blurred vision of the Baltimore girl.

original: Some of these prisoners were men of title who should be above such mean occupation. They were grieved by the indignity and mourned for their neglected farms. As they cut grass in the morning the younger men sang in time with the strokes of their machetes: "Kotma of the ashy buttocks, He is fit to be a slave. The white man has no sense, He is fit to be a slave." The court messengers did not like to be called Ashy-Buttocks, and they beat the men. But the song spread in Umuofia. Okonkwo's head was bowed in sadness as Obierika told him these things.
paraphrase: A number of these incarcerated individuals were gentlemen of high social standing, whose positions ought to have exempted them from such menial labor. They felt deeply distressed by this affront to their dignity and lamented the state of their abandoned farmlands. While they were engaged in cutting grass during the morning hours, the younger prisoners rhythmically chanted a song in unison with the swings of their machetes: "Kotma with the ashen buttocks, He is only suited for servitude. The Caucasian man lacks understanding, He is only suited for servitude." The messengers of the court were displeased to be referred to as Ashy-Buttocks, and consequently, they physically assaulted the men. However, the tune became widely known throughout Umuofia. Okonkwo's head drooped with sorrow as Obierika recounted these events to him.

original: Doctor Cornelius quickly cut up the remains of a cold chicken and some slices of venison and put them, with bread and an apple or so and a little flask of good wine, into the wallet which he then gave to Caspian. It fitted on by a strap over Caspian’s shoulder, like a satchel you would use for taking books to school. That’s right. And now we must go to the Great Tower and talk.”   When they had reached the top of the tower (it was a cloudy night, not at all like the night when they had seen the conjunction of Tarva and Alambil) Doctor Cornelius said,   “Dear Prince, you must leave this castle at once and go to seek your fortune in the wide world. Your life is in danger here.”   “Why?” asked Caspian. Long life to your Majesty”—and suddenly, to Caspian’s great surprise, the little man dropped down on one knee and kissed his hand. Everyone except your Majesty knows that Miraz is a usurper. When he first began to rule he did not even pretend to be the King:, he called himself Lord Protector. But then your royal mother died, the good Queen and the only Telmarine who was ever kind to me. And then, one by one, all the great lords, who had known your father, died or disappeared. Not by accident, either. Miraz weeded them out. Belisar and Uvilas were shot with arrows on a hunting party: by chance, it was pretended.
paraphrase:
Doctor Cornelius promptly dissected the leftover cold chicken and some cuts of venison. He then placed these, along with bread, approximately one apple, and a small container of fine wine, into a satchel. This satchel was subsequently handed to Caspian. It was designed to be worn with a strap over Caspian's shoulder, much like a bag one might carry for schoolbooks. "Excellent," he remarked. "Now, we must proceed to the Great Tower for a discussion."

Upon their arrival at the pinnacle of the tower—it was an overcast evening, distinctly different from the clear night when they had observed the alignment of Tarva and Alambil—Doctor Cornelius declared, "Esteemed Prince, you are compelled to depart this fortress immediately and venture into the expansive world to pursue your destiny. Your existence here is imperiled."

Caspian inquired, "For what reason?" Doctor Cornelius then unexpectedly knelt and pressed his lips to Caspian's hand, proclaiming, "May your Majesty enjoy a long reign." To Caspian's utter astonishment, the diminutive man performed this gesture. "Everyone, with the exception of your Majesty, is aware that Miraz is an illegitimate ruler. Initially, when he assumed power, he didn't even feign to be the monarch; he identified himself as the Lord Protector. However, following the demise of your royal mother, the benevolent Queen and the sole Telmarine who ever showed me kindness, one after another, all the prominent noblemen who had been acquainted with your father either perished or vanished. These occurrences were not coincidental, I assure you. Miraz systematically eliminated them. Belisar and Uvilas, for instance, were struck by arrows during a hunting expedition; this was, it was asserted, purely by chance."

original: “Ha. Good one.” He shrugged out of the jacket as if my glance had reminded him he still had it on, and hung it on the peg reserved for his gear. His gun belt was already slung in place—he hadn’t felt the need to wear that to the station for a few weeks. There had been no more disturbing disappearances to trouble the small town of Forks, Washington, no more sightings of the giant, mysterious wolves in the ever-rainy woods . . .

original: Instead she looked away and licked her lips a little before nodding her head. I see,' she said. He's not a doctor, is he?'   'No,' said Maria, shaking her head. He's a waiter.'   'I knew it,' said Bruno, feeling very pleased with himself. In another life. Before he came here.'   Bruno frowned and thought about it. But she was telling me about this man Pavel who chops our vegetables for us and waits on table. I think he lives on your side of the fence.'   Shmuel looked up for a moment and stopped eating. Do you know him? He's very old and has a white jacket that he wears when he's serving dinner. You've probably seen him.'   'No,' said Shmuel, shaking his head. Anyway, the reason I wanted to tell you about him is because he's from Poland too. Like you.'   'Most of us here are from Poland,' said Shmuel. Anyway, he was a doctor in his home town before he came here but he's not allowed to be a doctor any more and if Father had known that he had cleaned my knee when I hurt myself then there would have been trouble.'   'The soldiers don't normally like people getting better,' said Shmuel, swallowing the last piece of bread. After a few moments he looked through the wire and asked another question that had been preying on his mind. I don't think he's a good soldier at all. I mean one like Father. One of the good soldiers.'   'There aren't any good soldiers,' said Shmuel.
paraphrase:
Instead, she averted her gaze and lightly moistened her lips prior to giving a nod of affirmation. "I understand," she uttered. "He's not a physician, is he?"

"No," Maria responded, shaking her head from side to side. "He is a server."

"I was certain of it," Bruno declared, experiencing a surge of self-satisfaction. "In a previous existence. Prior to his arrival here."

Bruno furrowed his brow and contemplated this information. "However, she was recounting details to me about this individual named Pavel, who prepares our vegetables and serves at the table. I believe he resides on your side of the barrier."

Shmuel briefly elevated his gaze and ceased his consumption. "Are you acquainted with him? He is quite elderly and dons a white coat when he is serving meals. You have likely encountered him."

"No," Shmuel replied, moving his head negatively. "Regardless, the motive behind my desire to inform you about him is that he also originates from Poland. Similar to you."

"A majority of us present here are Polish," Shmuel stated. "In any case, he practiced medicine in his native town before relocating here, but he is no longer permitted to act as a doctor, and if Father had been aware that he tended to my knee when I sustained an injury, then there would have been considerable difficulty."

"The military personnel typically do not favor individuals recovering," Shmuel commented, ingesting the final morsel of bread. After a brief interval, he peered through the wire mesh and posed another inquiry that had been occupying his thoughts. "I do not perceive him as a commendable soldier whatsoever. I mean, one akin to Father. One of the admirable soldiers."

"There are no admirable soldiers," Shmuel asserted.

original: I didn't see no di'monds, and I told Tom Sawyer so. He said there was loads of them there, anyway; and he said there was A-rabs there, too, and elephants and things. I said, why couldn't we see them, then? He said if I warn't so ignorant, but had read a book called Don Quixote, I would know without asking. He said it was all done by enchantment. He said there was hundreds of soldiers there, and elephants and treasure, and so on, but we had enemies which he called magicians; and they had turned the whole thing into an infant Sunday- school, just out of spite.

original: And it began to shake and rattle, like something agitating with a metallic life. He took the first of the empty stools and the eight other egwugwu began to sit in order of seniority after him. Okonkwo's wives, and perhaps other women as well, might have noticed that the second egwugwu had the springy walk of Okonkwo. And they might also have noticed that Okonkwo was not among the titled men and elders who sat behind the row of egwugwu. But if they thought these things they kept them within themselves. The egwugwu with the springy walk was one of the dead fathers of the clan. He looked terrible with the smoked raffia "body, a huge wooden face painted white except for the round hollow eyes and the charred teeth that were as big as a man's fingers. On his head were two powerful horns. When all the egwugwu had sat down and the sound of the many tiny bells and rattles on their bodies had subsided, Evil Forest addressed the two groups of people facing them. "Uzowulu's body, I salute you," he said. Spirits always addressed humans as "bodies." Uzowulu bent down and touched the earth with his right hand as a sign of submission. "Our father, my hand has touched the ground," he said. "Uzowulu's body, do you know me?" asked the spirit. "How can I know you, father? You are beyond our knowledge." Evil Forest then turned to the other group and addressed the eldest of the three brothers.
paraphrase: Then, a shaking and rattling commenced, resembling an object imbued with a mechanical vitality. He occupied the foremost empty stool, and the subsequent eight egwugwu arranged themselves in descending order of precedence following him. It is conceivable that Okonkwo's wives, and potentially other women, might have observed that the second egwugwu possessed Okonkwo's characteristic energetic gait. Furthermore, they might have also noted Okonkwo's absence from the assembly of titled men and elders positioned behind the line of egwugwu. Were you onstage? Oh, God, no. Never. I was on the studio floor. I somehow missed that episode, but it sounds awesome. And Im not even a person who wore out her Supremes Greatest Hits tape in grade school. I laughedalthough wed discussed it less than three days ago, I certainly wouldnt have expected him to remember that detail from my lifeand he added, My greatest show was in Glasgow, during a crazy summer storm. For the entire last hour, there was a torrential downpour, and everyone and everything got soaked. Me, my band, the instruments, the stage, the audience. I ruined my guitar, and it was so completely worth it. I guess the common denominator for epic live performances is a weather event involving precipitation, I said, and he grinned again. And it doesnt even have to be natural, he said. It can be man-made. Have you really never appeared onstage here? Yes, I said. I really never have. I prefer lurking in the shadows like a goblin. He made a concerned expression, and I said, I dont mean goblin in a bad way. Its a point of pride. His expression shifted to something warmer as he said, Goblin definitely isnt the word Id have picked for you.

Myself, my musical ensemble, the instruments, the performance area, the spectators. My guitar was destroyed, and it was entirely worthwhile." It's actually a source of personal satisfaction." His expression softened into something more amiable as he commented, " 'Mythical creature' is certainly not the term I would have chosen to describe you."

original: He has found a way out. Tiny dot on the map, near Naumburg, in Saxony. Two hundred miles east. Only in his most intrepid dreams did he allow himself to hope that he might travel so far. He carries the sheet of paper in a daze to the alley where Frau Elena boils sheets amid billows of steam. They’ve already paid the fare.”   “When?”   “Two weeks.”   Frau Elena: strands of hair stuck to her cheeks, maroon aprons under her eyes, pink rims around her nostrils. Thin crucifix against her damp throat. Is she proud? She rubs her eyes and nods absently. The neighbors.” She laughs a sudden and startling laugh. The man who took your book.”   “Not Jutta.”   “No. Not Jutta.”   He rehearses in his head the argument he will present to his sister. It means duty. Obligation. Every German fulfilling his function. Put on your boots and go to work. But before the girls arrive, news of his acceptance has reverberated through the block. Neighbors come  over one after another and exclaim and wag their chins. Coal wives bring pig knuckles and cheese; they pass around Werner’s acceptance letter; the ones who can read, read it aloud to the ones who cannot, and Jutta comes home to a crowded, exhilarated room. The twins—Hannah and Susanne Gerlitz—sprint laps around the sofa, looped up in the excitement, and six-year-old Rolf Hupfauer sings  Rise! Rise! All glory to the fatherland!  and several of the other children join in, and Werner doesn’t see Frau Elena speak to Jutta in the corner of the parlor, doesn’t see Jutta run upstairs.

He carries the document, in a state of bewilderment, towards the narrow passage where Frau Elena is engaged in the task of boiling linens amidst voluminous clouds of vapor. They have already settled the cost of the journey.” “At what time?” “In two weeks.” Frau Elena: individual threads of hair adhered to the sides of her face, dark red discolorations beneath her ocular organs, reddish margins encircling her nasal orifices. A slender crucifix rested against her moist neck. Does she experience a sense of pride? She gently rubs her eyes and gives an absentminded affirmation. “The individuals residing nearby.” She emits an abrupt and surprising peal of laughter. “The gentleman who acquired your publication.” “Not Jutta.” “Indeed. Not Jutta.” He mentally goes over the reasoning he intends to put forth to his sibling. It signifies a responsibility. An imperative. Each German citizen performing their designated role. Wives of coal miners bring portions of pig knuckles and various types of cheese; they circulate Werner’s notification of acceptance; those who possess literacy skills enunciate its contents for the benefit of those who lack them, and Jutta returns to a congested, overjoyed chamber. The twin siblings—Hannah and Susanne Gerlitz—race in circuits around the upholstered seating, propelled by the surge of enthusiasm, and Rolf Hupfauer, a child of six years, vocalizes “Ascend! Ascend! original: Come and sit down with us, Miss. Minnie patted the seat next to her, gazing at the teacher as she had done over sixty years ago. Id love to. Miss Hamilton eased herself slowly towards the table. The scooter is parked outside. I dont get around as well as I used to: my arthritis is very annoying, but I have a good doctor and she keeps an eye on me. Thats why Ive moved here. Ive just bought a nice bungalow on Tadderly Road. She sat down carefully, Minnie pushing out the chair, extending a hand. Well, how pleasant. Just like old times. Youre most welcome. Odile offered a wide smile. What can I get you? Tea Miss Hamilton ignored the menu that Odile offered. And did I overhear someone say carrot cake? A slice of that would be wonderful. With a dollop of cream, if you have it. She turned to the three former pupils who sat at the table looking at her, their eyes shining. This is nice, isnt it? Its been quite a while She leaned forwards, her voice soft with warmth. Well, you must tell me about everything youve been doing over the last sixty years. It will be good to catch up with all the news. Then she stared across the caf and she was suddenly stern. Please do close your mouth, Jimmy Baker. Youre staring again, and you know its impolite to stare. Yes, Miss, Jimmy replied automatically, and Dangerous Dave dug him in the ribs and began to laugh.
paraphrase:
"Please, join us, Miss." Minnie gently tapped the seat beside her, her gaze fixed on the teacher with the same expression she had worn more than six decades prior. "I'd be delighted to." Miss Hamilton carefully maneuvered herself towards the table. "My scooter is just outside. My mobility isn't what it once was; my arthritis is quite bothersome, though I have an excellent doctor who monitors me closely. That's actually why I've relocated here. I've recently purchased a lovely bungalow on Tadderly Road." She settled herself cautiously, Minnie extending a hand to help push in her chair.

"Well, isn't this delightful? It's just like the old days. You are absolutely welcome here." Odile offered a broad smile. "What can I bring you?"

"Tea," Miss Hamilton stated, disregarding the menu Odile presented. "And did I happen to catch someone mentioning carrot cake? A slice of that would be splendid, with a dollop of cream if available." She then turned to the three former students who were seated at the table, their eyes bright as they looked at her. "This is lovely, isn't it? It's been quite some time." She leaned forward, her voice softened by genuine warmth. "Now, you simply must fill me in on all that you've been doing these past sixty years. It will be wonderful to catch up on all the developments."

Then, her gaze shifted across the café, and her demeanor abruptly became stern. "Jimmy Baker, please do close your mouth. You are staring again, and you are well aware that staring is impolite."

"Yes, Miss," Jimmy responded instinctively, as 'Dangerous Dave' nudged him in the ribs and started to laugh.

Heavy white silk with a low neckline and tight waist and sleeves that fall from my wrists to the floor. And pearls. Everywhere pearls. Stitched into the dress and in ropes at my throat and forming the crown for the veil.

original: “The city is the place where today live the people of God, of whom you, we, are the shepherds. It is the place of scandal in which the rich prelates preach virtue to poor and hungry people. The Patarine disorders were born of this situation. They are sad, but not incomprehensible. The Catharists are something else. That is an Oriental heresy, outside the doctrine of the church.
paraphrase: "The metropolis serves as the dwelling place for God's flock today, a flock for whom you and we serve as guides. It is a setting ripe for moral outrage, where wealthy ecclesiastical leaders lecture impoverished and famished individuals on upright conduct. The upheavals caused by the Patarine movement emerged from this very circumstance. These events are regrettable, yet they are not beyond understanding. The Catharists, however, represent a distinct phenomenon. Their beliefs constitute an Eastern heresy, divergent from the established teachings of the church.

original: "I shall hear about you all," said he; that is my chief consolation. I shall hear of every thing that is going on among you. I have engaged Mrs. Weston to correspond with me. She has been so kind as to promise it. Oh! the blessing of a female correspondent, when one is really interested in the absent!--she will tell me every thing. In her letters I shall be at dear Highbury again." A very friendly shake of the hand, a very earnest "Good-bye," closed the speech, and the door had soon shut out Frank Churchill. Short had been the notice--short their meeting; he was gone; and Emma felt so sorry to part, and foresaw so great a loss to their little society from his absence as to begin to be afraid of being too sorry, and feeling it too much.
paraphrase: "I will be informed about all of you," he stated; "that is my primary comfort. I will be apprised of every event unfolding in your lives. I have secured Mrs. Weston's agreement to exchange letters with me. She has graciously consented to this promise. Oh! the immense benefit of having a female correspondent, especially when one genuinely cares about someone who is away!—she will disclose everything to me. Through her letters, I will feel as if I am back in beloved Highbury once more." A heartfelt handshake and a sincere "Good-bye" brought his discourse to a close, and the door swiftly concealed Frank Churchill from view. The warning had been brief—their encounter fleeting; he had departed; and Emma experienced such profound sorrow at their separation, and anticipated such a significant void within their small community due to his absence, that she began to worry about feeling too much regret and being overly distressed.

The counterfeiters duplicated and sold phonograph records of famous artists, packaging everything so skillfully they were never caught. Naturally on the records they sold to stores the artists and original production company received not a penny.

original: Her mysterious pen pal had written again, even though she had yet to respond to their myth-filled letter. She was beginning to wonder if they were from another time. Perhaps they had lived in this very room, long before her. Perhaps they were destined to live here, years from now. Perhaps their letters were somehow slipping through a fissure of time, but it was this place that was causing it. Iris retrieved the paper and sat on the edge of her bed, reading: Do you ever feel as if you wear armor, day after day? That when people look at you, they see only the shine of steel that youve so carefully encased yourself in? They see what they want to see in youthe warped reflection of their own face, or a piece of the sky, or a shadow cast between buildings.

original: Then his eyes touched on the book under Montag's arm and he did not look so old any more and not quite as fragile. Slowly his fear went. "I'm sorry. One has to be careful." He looked at the book under Montag's arm and could not stop. "So it's true." Montag stepped inside. The door shut. "Sit down." Faber backed up, as if he feared the book might vanish if he took his eyes from it. Behind him, the door to a bedroom stood open, and in that room a litter of machinery and steel tools was strewn upon a desk-top. Montag had only a glimpse, before Faber, seeing Montag's attention diverted, turned quickly and shut the bedroom door and stood holding the knob with a trembling hand. His gaze returned unsteadily to Montag, who was now seated with the book in his lap. "The book-where did you-?" "I stole it." Faber, for the first time, raised his eyes and looked directly into Montag's face. "You're brave." "No," said Montag. "My wife's dying. A friend of mine's already dead. Someone who may have been a friend was burnt less than twenty-four hours ago. You're the only one I knew might help me. To see. To see. ." Faber's hands itched on his knees. "May I?" "Sorry." Montag gave him the book. "It's been a long time. I'm not a religious man. But it's been a long time." Faber turned the pages, stopping here and there to read.
paraphrase: As his gaze fell upon the book tucked beneath Montag's arm, the lines of age seemed to soften on his face, and his former frailty appeared less pronounced. Gradually, his apprehension dissipated. "My apologies. One must exercise caution," he murmured. His eyes remained fixed on the book under Montag's arm, unable to break away. "So, it is true then." Montag entered the room, and the door closed behind him. "Please, have a seat." Faber retreated a step, as if the book might dematerialize if he averted his gaze even for an instant. Behind him, the entrance to a bedroom lay ajar, and within that space, a jumble of mechanical devices and metal implements was scattered across a desk. Montag caught only a fleeting impression before Faber, noticing Montag's attention wander, swiftly turned and closed the bedroom door, then stood gripping the doorknob with a tremor in his hand. His eyes, wavering slightly, found Montag again, who was now seated with the book resting in his lap. "The book—where did you obtain it—?" "I purloined it." For the first time, Faber lifted his eyes and met Montag's gaze directly. "You possess courage." "No," Montag countered. "My spouse is in her final moments. A companion of mine has already perished. An individual who might have been an ally was incinerated less than a full day ago. You are the sole person I believed capable of assisting me. To comprehend. To comprehend. . ." Faber's hands fidgeted on his knees. "May I?" he inquired. "Forgive me," Montag responded, handing him the book. "It has been an extended period. I do not consider myself a person of faith. Yet, it has truly been a very long time." Faber began to turn the leaves of the book, pausing at various points to peruse the text.

original: Bartleby, said I, in the kindest tone I could assume under such exciting circumstances, will you go home with me nownot to my office, but my dwellingand remain there till we can conclude upon some convenient arrangement for you at our leisure? Come, let us start now, right away. No: at present I would prefer not to make any change at all. I answered nothing; but effectually dodging every one by the suddenness and rapidity of my flight, rushed from the building, ran up Wall-street towards Broadway, and jumping into the first omnibus was soon removed from pursuit. As soon as tranquility returned I distinctly perceived that I had now done all that I possibly could, both in respect to the demands of the landlord and his tenants, and with regard to my own desire and sense of duty, to benefit Bartleby, and shield him from rude persecution.
paraphrase: "Bartleby," I uttered, using the most gentle voice I could muster given the intense situation, "would you be willing to accompany me home now—not to my workplace, but to my residence—and stay there until we can, at our convenience, finalize a suitable plan for you? Come, let's depart immediately, without delay." He responded, "No: currently, I would rather not alter anything whatsoever." I offered no reply; instead, by swiftly and abruptly evading everyone present, I bolted from the structure, sprinted up Wall Street in the direction of Broadway, and quickly boarded the nearest omnibus, thus rapidly escaping any chase. Once my composure was regained, I clearly recognized that I had exhausted all possible efforts, both in addressing the requirements of the landlord and his occupants, and in fulfilling my personal wish and moral obligation to assist Bartleby and safeguard him from harsh mistreatment.

original: We don’t live in a fairy tale. Come along, Dante. If we’re reforging this knife, we should prepare.” She’s still chuckling under her breath at her own joke as Dante, the soldier with the wicked eyes, shuts the panel in the wall behind them. When he turns, a muscle twitches in his jaw. Stop it. You and Alice mean the world to me. I want this to work out between us, but if I don’t go and see this Sorcerer hiding out on the Ninth, only one of us is going to survive to see next week and it won’t be you or me. I have to try,” I say firmly, take Alice’s hand, and follow Mila and Clair through the panel into the dark between the walls. Leaving Mila and Clair behind, we creep to the library. We don’t speak as we walk; it’s enough to be together, alone and with freedom so close we can almost taste it. Compared to the Gilded, the people he’s killed. All the families he’s destroyed, an entire continent laid to waste. The Resistance will end him.”   “He was always going to fail.” Alice pauses at the foot of the steps to the Ninth and smiles at the maelstrom of shadows at the top. Except one.”   The end where you let her go. I want to ask Alice what he means, but I’m too scared to talk. How does it feel to lay the paths before their feet and watch them walk? Do you wonder, Alice, if they would walk the same way if you did not give them a map?   The voice of the Sorcerer.
paraphrase:
This isn't some fantastical storybook reality. Come with me, Dante. If we are indeed reshaping this blade, then we must make ready." She continues to quietly amuse herself with her own quip as Dante, the warrior with the piercing gaze, secures the wall panel behind them. A muscle in his jaw spasms when he pivots. "Enough. You and Alice are everything to me. I deeply desire for things to succeed between us, but if I do not seek out this Sorcerer who is concealing himself on the Ninth, only one of us will remain alive by next week, and it won't be you or me. I am compelled to make this attempt," I state resolutely, grasp Alice's hand, and proceed to trail Mila and Clair through the panel into the concealed darkness within the walls.

After parting ways with Mila and Clair, we stealthily advance towards the library. We maintain our silence as we traverse; simply being together, in solitude, with the prospect of liberty so near it feels tangible, is sufficient. "In contrast to the Gilded, consider the individuals he has murdered. All the families he has devastated, an entire land reduced to ruin. The Resistance will bring about his downfall." "He was always destined to fail." Alice pauses at the base of the staircase leading to the Ninth and offers a smile at the swirling chaos of shadows at its summit. "Except for one."

The conclusion where you release her. I long to inquire of Alice what his words signify, but fear prevents me from speaking. What sensation accompanies the act of charting courses before their very feet and observing their journey? Do you ponder, Alice, if they would follow the identical route had you not provided them with a guide?

The utterance of the Sorcerer.

original: I never thought about crystals as a weakness before. It explains why Evelyn laughed so hard the afternoon they persuaded me to join the Resistance. It feels so long ago. I’ve gained a whole new identity as the Warden’s Death-Weaver since then. “The gilding severs the Gilded’s connection to their souls, right? So, if they had crystals, we might be able to use them like we do when we walk in Death and anchor their souls back in their bodies.” I shake my head a little and pick at my skirt.
paraphrase: Prior to this, I had never considered crystals to be a vulnerability. This realization clarifies why Evelyn found it so amusing the day they convinced me to become a part of the Resistance. It seems like ages have passed since then. In the intervening time, I've adopted an entirely new persona as the Warden's Death-Weaver. "The gilding process disconnects the Gilded from their souls, doesn't it? Therefore, if they possessed crystals, we could potentially utilize them in the same manner we do when we traverse Death and re-establish their souls within their physical forms." I subtly move my head from side to side and fidget with the fabric of my skirt.

But what most struck Winston was the look of helpless fright on the woman's greyish face. Back in the flat he stepped quickly past the telescreen and sat down at the table again, still rubbing his neck.

original: “I used to be ashamed. All the time. Because I’m a girl, because I’m a bastard and my mother was . . . a lot of things. I wanted admiration. Respect. Something to prove that my life was more than just a pitiable accident. I chased after someone . . .” I break off and squeeze my hands together to gather myself. “I don’t have a prominent family name, or really any kind of traditional family at all. I’m not beautiful, I’m not even particularly charming. All I have ever had is my stubbornness and my talent. I wrote my way out of  hell. And so now I am proud. I am proud, because I did that, and no one can ever take it from me. Even if I am the only one who ever knows it, that is enough.”
paraphrase: "I was perpetually embarrassed. Constantly. Due to being female, due to being illegitimate, and because my mother was... well, many things. I yearned for adoration. Esteem. Something to demonstrate that my existence was more significant than merely a regrettable mistake. I pursued an individual..." I pause, clasping my hands tightly together to compose myself. "I lack a distinguished family surname, or, in fact, any conventional family whatsoever. I am not attractive, nor am I especially captivating. All I have ever possessed are my unwavering determination and my innate ability. I utilized my writing to escape a terrible situation. Consequently, I am now filled with self-respect. I am proud, because I accomplished that feat, and no one will ever be able to diminish it. Even if I am the sole person who ever acknowledges it, that is entirely sufficient."

original: I-I  I was so confused. I reached into my pocket and pulled out Janes letter. Checking the date, I saw that it was written over a week ago. Why wasnt I informed? Oh, were you not? Im sure I told Nurse Patricia to pass the message along. I looked down at the letter, the words swimming in front of me. My hands began to shake with a rage that boiled inside of me.
paraphrase: I-I was utterly bewildered. My hand instinctively went to my pocket, retrieving Jane's letter. Upon examining the date, I noticed it had been penned more than seven days prior. "How was I not notified?" I questioned. "Oh, you weren't?" came the reply. "I'm quite certain I instructed Nurse Patricia to relay the information." original: Hovering in the hallway, empty tray in hand, she strained to hear the rest of their evil plans. She knew the roundups were to take place, she knew where the women and children would be taken, at least initially, but she didn’t know the ultimate destination for all those innocent souls. Or the dates. She heard the rustle of papers being handed off from one man to another. Every detail would be written on those pages. She would have to wait until the household was asleep and, slim though the possibility was, pray the documents didn’t walk out the door with von Bauer’s superior officer.
paraphrase: With an empty tray held in her hand, she lingered in the corridor, trying intently to catch the remaining details of their sinister schemes. She was aware that the roundups were imminent and knew the initial destination for the women and children, but the final fate of those innocent individuals remained a mystery to her. The specific dates were also unknown. The faint sound of documents being exchanged between two men reached her ears. All the crucial information, she realized, would be inscribed on those very sheets. Her only recourse was to bide her time until everyone in the household had retired for the night and, despite the slim odds, hope that the documents wouldn't leave the premises with von Bauer’s commanding officer.

original: It made me uncomfortable. I donned my jacket—which had the feel of a biohazard suit—and headed out into the rain. The sloshing of my new waterproof boots was unnerving. I missed the normal crunch of gravel as I walked. I couldn’t pause and admire my truck again as I wanted; I was in a hurry to get out of the misty wet that swirled around my head and clung to my hair under my hood. Either Billy or Charlie had obviously cleaned it up, but the tan upholstered seats still smelled faintly of tobacco, gasoline, and peppermint. The engine started quickly, to my relief, but loudly, roaring to life and then idling at top volume. Well, a truck this old was bound to have a flaw. The antique radio worked, a plus that I hadn’t expected. The school was, like most other things, just off the highway. It was not obvious that it was a school; only the sign, which declared it to be the Forks High School, made me stop. It looked like a collection of matching houses, built with maroon-colored bricks. There were so many trees and shrubs I couldn’t see its size at first. Where was the feel of the institution? I wondered nostalgically. Where were the chain-link fences, the metal detectors?   I parked in front of the first building, which had a small sign over the door reading  FRONT OFFICE . No one else was parked there, so I was sure it was off limits, but I decided I would get directions inside instead of circling around in the rain like an idiot.
paraphrase:
A sense of unease settled over me. I pulled on my jacket, which felt akin to wearing a biohazard suit, and stepped outside into the downpour. The squishing sound of my new waterproof boots was disquieting; I longed for the familiar crunch of gravel beneath my feet. I couldn't afford to linger and gaze at my truck as I wished; I was eager to escape the swirling, damp mist that enveloped my head and clung to my hair beneath my hood. It was clear that either Billy or Charlie had cleaned the vehicle, yet a faint scent of tobacco, gasoline, and peppermint still lingered on the tan upholstered seats. To my relief, the engine ignited promptly, but with a forceful roar that then settled into a high-volume idle. Well, a truck of this age was bound to possess some imperfection. The antique radio, unexpectedly, functioned, which was a bonus.

The school, like most other establishments, was situated just off the highway. Its appearance didn't immediately suggest it was an educational facility; only the sign proclaiming "Forks High School" prompted me to halt. It resembled a cluster of identical houses, constructed from maroon-colored bricks. The abundance of trees and shrubs initially obscured my view of its full scale. "Where was the characteristic atmosphere of an institution?" I mused with a touch of nostalgia. "Where were the chain-link fences, the metal detectors?" I pulled into a parking spot directly in front of the initial building, which bore a small sign above its entrance stating "FRONT OFFICE." Since no other vehicles were parked there, I suspected it was off-limits, but I resolved to go inside for directions rather than foolishly driving in circles in the rain.

original: The only way is to choose the tallest tree that overhangs the path.”   Of course “somebody” meant Bilbo. They chose him, because to be of any use the climber must get his head above the topmost leaves, and so he must be light enough for the highest and slenderest branches to bear him. Poor Mr. Baggins had never had much practice in climbing trees, but they hoisted him up into the lowest branches of an enormous oak that grew right out into the path, and up he had to go as best he could. He pushed his way through the tangled twigs with many a slap in the eye; he was greened and grimed from the old bark of the greater boughs; more than once he slipped and caught himself just in time; and at last, after a dreadful struggle in a difficult place where there seemed to be no convenient branches at all, he got near the top. All the time he was wondering whether there were spiders in the tree, and how he was going to get down again (except by falling). But they were only small ones of ordinary size, and they were after the butterflies. Bilbo’s eyes were nearly blinded by the light. He could hear the dwarves shouting up at him from far below, but he could not answer, only hold on and blink. The sun was shining brilliantly, and it was a long while before he could bear it. When he could, he saw all round him a sea of dark green, ruffled here and there by the breeze; and there were everywhere hundreds of butterflies.

He forced his way through the intertwined twigs, frequently getting slapped in the face; he became stained green and dirty from the ancient bark of the larger branches; on multiple occasions, he lost his grip but managed to recover just in the nick of time; and finally, following a terrible exertion in a challenging section where suitable branches appeared nonexistent, he approached the summit. Throughout this ordeal, he contemplated the possibility of spiders residing in the tree, and how he would manage to descend (other than by plummeting). However, they were merely small, typical-sized spiders, and their focus was on the butterflies. Bilbo's vision was almost obscured by the intense illumination. He could discern the dwarves yelling upwards at him from a considerable distance below, but he was unable to respond, capable only of clinging on and blinking. The sun was shining with remarkable intensity, and a significant period passed before he could endure its brilliance. Once he could, he observed an expanse of deep green surrounding him, intermittently stirred by the gentle wind; and everywhere, there were countless butterflies.

Yossarian choked on his toast and eggs at the enormity of his error in tearing her long, lithe, nude, young vibrant limbs into any pieces of paper so impudently and dumping her down so smugly into the gutter from the curb. He felt an urgent desire to be alone with her again soon and sprang up impetuously from his table and went running outside and back down the street toward the apartment in search of the tiny bits of paper in the gutter, but they had all been flushed away by a street cleaner’s hose.

original: In Lodge's strange romance "A Margarite of America" it was stated that in the chamber of Margarite were seen "all the chaste ladies of the world, inchased out of silver, looking through fair mirrours of chrysolites, carbuncles, sapphires, and greene emeraults." Marco Polo had watched the inhabitants of Zipangu place a rose-colored pearl in the mouth of the dead. A sea-monster had been enamoured of the pearl that the diver brought to King Perozes, and had slain the thief, and mourned for seven moons over his loss. When the Huns lured the king into the great pit, he flung it away,-- Procopius tells the story,--nor was it ever found again, though the Emperor Anastasius offered five hundred-weight of gold pieces for it. The King of Malabar had shown a Venetian a rosary of one hundred and four pearls, one for every god that he worshipped. When the Duke de Valentinois, son of Alexander VI., visited Louis XII. of France, his horse was loaded with gold leaves, according to Brantme, and his cap had double rows of rubies that threw out a great light. Charles of England had ridden in stirrups hung with three hundred and twenty-one diamonds. Richard II. had a coat, valued at thirty thousand marks, which was covered with balas rubies. Hall described Henry VIII., on his way to the Tower previous to his coronation, as wearing "a jacket of raised gold, the placard embroidered with diamonds and other rich stones, and a great bauderike about his neck of large balasses.

Marco Polo had previously witnessed the residents of Zipangu inserting a pearl, colored like a rose, into the mouths of their deceased. A creature from the ocean depths had become captivated by a pearl, which a diver had presented to King Perozes; this creature had then killed the individual who stole it and subsequently lamented its absence for a duration of seven lunar cycles. When the Huns enticed the king into a massive chasm, he cast the pearl away—a tale recounted by Procopius—and it was never recovered, despite Emperor Anastasius's offer of five hundred-weight of gold coins for its return. When the Duke de Valentinois, the offspring of Alexander VI., paid a visit to Louis XII. of France, his equine mount was laden with gold foliage, as recorded by Brantme, and his headwear featured two rows of rubies that emitted a brilliant radiance. Charles of England had once ridden with stirrups adorned with three hundred and twenty-one diamonds. Richard II. possessed a garment, appraised at thirty thousand marks, which was embellished with balas rubies. Hall depicted Henry VIII., en route to the Tower preceding his coronation, as attired in "a jacket of raised gold, its front panel adorned with diamonds and other precious gems, and a substantial shoulder-belt around his neck crafted from large balas rubies."

original: In the prescribed history approved by the Warden’s council, the gilding process was devised by the Sorcerer as a horrifying method of controlling magical prisoners without the waste of execution. But with his Gilded creations, the Sorcerer handed the Warden too much power, and so, the Warden exiled him into Death. Banishing a deity makes the High Warden’s reputation all the more terrifying, not that he needs it. But powerful as he is, he’s just a man. A man who wrote the history books. Anything can be created with paper and ink; a tale written down is made real by the generations who consume it. I’m not sure if the Sorcerer of fairy-tale fame ever existed at all. Or how the Warden could have banished him if he did.
paraphrase: According to the sanctioned historical account endorsed by the Warden’s council, the Sorcerer originated the gilding procedure as a horrific means of managing magical inmates, avoiding the need for executions. However, through his Gilded creations, the Sorcerer inadvertently granted the Warden excessive authority, leading to the Warden's decision to banish him into Death. The act of exiling a deity further enhances the High Warden’s already fearsome reputation, though he hardly requires it. Despite his immense power, he remains merely a man—a man responsible for authoring the historical records. Anything is capable of being fabricated with paper and ink; a narrative committed to writing becomes solidified into reality by the subsequent generations who absorb it. I question whether the Sorcerer, renowned in folklore, truly existed in the first place, or how the Warden could have possibly exiled him if such a being did indeed exist.

original: Colonel Cathcart clawed and elbowed his way through the unruly, bewildered mass until he stood outside by himself. He stared up at the sky in stark astonishment and horror. Milo’s planes, ballooning serenely in over the blossoming treetops with their bomb bay doors open and wing flaps down and with their monstrous, bug-eyed, blinding, fiercely flickering, eerie landing lights on, were the most apocalyptic sight he had ever beheld. Colonel Cathcart let go a stricken gasp of dismay and hurled himself headlong into his jeep, almost sobbing. He found the gas pedal and the ignition and sped toward the airfield as fast as the rocking car would carry him, his huge flabby hands clenched and bloodless on the wheel or blaring his horn tormentedly. Once he almost killed himself when he swerved with a banshee screech of tires to avoid plowing into a bunch of men running crazily toward the hills in their underwear with their stunned faces down and their thin arms pressed high around their temples as puny shields. Yellow, orange and red fires were burning on both sides of the road. Tents and trees were in flames, and Milo’s planes kept coming around interminably with their blinking white landing lights on and their bomb bay doors open. Colonel Cathcart almost turned the jeep over when he slammed the brakes on at the control tower. He leaped from the car while it was still skidding dangerously and hurtled up the flight of steps inside, where three men were busy at the instruments and the controls. He bowled two of them aside in his lunge for the nickel-plated microphone, his eyes glittering wildly and his beefy face contorted with stress. He squeezed the microphone in a bestial grip and began shouting hysterically at the top of his voice.
paraphrase: Colonel Cathcart, with considerable effort, pushed and shoved his way through the chaotic, bewildered crowd until he emerged alone outside. He gazed upwards at the sky, filled with utter shock and dread. Milo's aircraft, which were floating gracefully above the blooming treetops with their bomb bay doors open, wing flaps extended, and their enormous, bulging-eyed, dazzling, intensely flickering, unsettling landing lights illuminated, presented the most catastrophic spectacle he had ever witnessed. Colonel Cathcart emitted a pained gasp of distress and threw himself headfirst into his jeep, nearly weeping. He located the accelerator pedal and the ignition, then sped towards the airfield as rapidly as the swaying vehicle could take him, his large, soft hands clenched and pale on the steering wheel, or frantically blaring his horn. At one point, he narrowly avoided a fatal accident when he swerved with a piercing screech of tires to prevent crashing into a group of men who were sprinting wildly towards the hills in their undergarments, their stunned faces averted downwards and their slender arms pressed high around their temples like meager shields. Vivid yellow, orange, and red flames were consuming everything on both sides of the thoroughfare. Tents and trees were engulfed in fire, and Milo's planes continued their endless circuits with their blinking white landing lights on and their bomb bay doors agape. Colonel Cathcart almost overturned the jeep when he abruptly applied the brakes at the control tower. He sprang from the vehicle while it was still skidding perilously and rushed up the staircase inside, where three individuals were occupied with the instruments and controls. He knocked two of them out of his path in his desperate lunge for the nickel-plated microphone, his eyes gleaming erratically and his fleshy face twisted with tension. He clutched the microphone with an animalistic grip and commenced screaming hysterically at the maximum volume of his voice.

original: The next morning he took me into the yard and gave me a good grooming, and just as I was going into my box, with my coat soft and bright, the Squire came in to look at me, and seemed pleased. “John,” he said, “I meant to have tried the new horse this morning, but I have other business. You may as well take him around after breakfast; go by the common and the Highwood, and back by the water-mill and the river, that will show his paces.”
paraphrase: The following morning, he led me out into the yard and meticulously groomed me. Just as I was about to enter my stall, my coat now gleaming and smooth, the Squire arrived to inspect me and appeared content. "John," he remarked, "I had intended to test out the new horse this morning, but I have other matters to attend to. You might as well lead him on a circuit after breakfast; proceed via the common and Highwood, returning past the water-mill and the river. That will allow us to observe his gaits."

original: You've been under a strain tonight no man should ever have to go through. Why you ain't in the bed from it I don't know, but I do know that for once you haven't been able to put two and two together, and we've got to settle this tonight because tomorrow'll be too late. Bob Ewell's got a kitchen knife in his craw." Mr. Tate added that Atticus wasn't going to stand there and maintain that any boy Jem's size with a busted arm had fight enough left in him to tackle and kill a grown man in the pitch dark. "Heck," said Atticus abruptly, "that was a switchblade you were waving. Where'd you get it?" "Took it off a drunk man," Mr. Tate answered coolly. I was trying to remember. Mr. Ewell was on me... then he went down.... Jem must have gotten up. At least I thought... "Heck?" "I said I took it off a drunk man downtown tonight. Ewell probably found that kitchen knife in the dump somewhere. Honed it down and bided his time... just bided his time." Atticus made his way to the swing and sat down. His hands dangled limply between his knees. He was looking at the floor. He had moved with the same slowness that night in front of the jail, when I thought it took him forever to fold his newspaper and toss it in his chair. Mr. Tate clumped softly around the porch.
paraphrase: Tonight, you've endured an immense hardship, a burden no individual should ever experience. The reason you're not in bed, recovering from it, eludes me, but I am certain that for a change, you haven't managed to connect the dots, and we absolutely must resolve this matter this evening, because waiting until tomorrow will be too late. Bob Ewell has a kitchen knife lodged in his stomach." Mr. Tate further asserted that Atticus was not going to remain there and argue that a boy of Jem's stature, with a broken arm, possessed sufficient fighting capability to confront and fatally injure an adult male in complete darkness. "Heck," Atticus interjected sharply, "the object you were brandishing was a switchblade. From where did you obtain it?" "I confiscated it from an inebriated individual," Mr. Tate replied calmly. I was attempting to recall the events. Mr. Ewell was upon me... then he collapsed.... Jem must have intervened. Or so I believed... "Heck?" "I stated that I removed it from a intoxicated man in the city center this evening. Ewell likely discovered that kitchen knife in a refuse pile somewhere. He sharpened it and patiently waited for the opportune moment... he simply waited for his chance." Atticus proceeded toward the swing and settled into it. His hands hung loosely between his knees. He was staring at the ground. He had moved with an identical lack of haste that night in front of the correctional facility, when I felt it took him an eternity to fold his newspaper and place it onto his chair. Mr. Tate moved quietly around the veranda.

original: Then she looked away, consulting a clipboard. What are we working with, Ezra? she said, making some inscrutable notes. She was tall and long-necked, supposedly one of the best at what she did in New York. I knew the exact measurements of my malady by heart. Twenty/one-twenty, farsighted, astigmatism in both eyes. Range of vision between one and ten feet. She nodded. So without the glasses you cant see anything closer than a foot? Or farther than ten, I said, annoyed at having to repeat myself. I have bifocals integrated into the lenses for the close-up stuff. Mm, she said, pitiless.
paraphrase: Next, she averted her gaze to examine a clipboard. "What exactly is our situation, Ezra?" she inquired, while scribbling some indecipherable marks. She possessed a tall stature and a lengthy neck, and was reputed to be among the top professionals in her field in New York. I had committed the precise dimensions of my visual impairment to memory: twenty/one-twenty, farsightedness, and astigmatism affecting both eyes, with a visual range spanning from one to ten feet. She gave a nod. "Therefore, without your spectacles, you are unable to perceive anything closer than a foot?" "Or beyond ten feet," I retorted, irritated by the need to reiterate the information. "My lenses incorporate bifocals for close-range tasks." "Hmm," she uttered, devoid of sympathy.

original: This isn’t going to be such fun.”   “At present,” said Lucy, “I want something to drink more than something to eat.”   Everyone else now felt thirsty, as one usually is after wading in salt water under a hot sun. We’d better go and look for them.”   “Does that mean we have to go back into all that thick wood?” said Susan. Edmund and Lucy wanted to leave them behind and do their exploring with bare feet, but Susan said this would be a mad thing to do. Except for an occasional seagull it was a very quiet place. The wood was so thick and tangled that they could hardly see into it at all; and nothing in it moved—not a bird, not even an insect. The children’s feet, after the change from the cool water, felt hot and heavy. Susan and Lucy had raincoats to carry. Edmund had put down his coat on the  station seat just before the magic overtook them, and he and Peter took it in turns to carry Peter’s greatcoat. About quarter of an hour later, after they had crossed a rocky ridge which ran out into a point, it made quite a sharp turn. Their backs were now to the part of the sea which had met them when they first came out of the wood, and now, looking ahead, they could see across the water another shore, thickly wooded like the one they were exploring. But in this they were disappointed.
paraphrase: "This isn't going to be particularly enjoyable," Lucy stated. "Right now, I'm more interested in having a drink than eating something." After spending time wading in salty water under intense sunlight, as is typical, everyone else also started to feel parched. "We should probably go and find them." Susan inquired, "Does that mean we have to re-enter that dense forest?" Edmund and Lucy were eager to leave their footwear behind and explore barefoot, but Susan argued that doing so would be unwise. Apart from the occasional seagull, the area was remarkably quiet. The forest was so dense and interwoven that visibility within it was almost nonexistent; nothing stirred inside – not a single bird, not even an insect. Following their transition from the refreshing water, the children's feet felt warm and heavy. Susan and Lucy were burdened with carrying their raincoats. Edmund had set his coat down on the station bench just moments before the enchantment enveloped them, and he and Peter shared the task of carrying Peter's large coat. Approximately fifteen minutes later, after traversing a rocky crest that extended into a point, the path took a rather abrupt turn. Their backs were now facing the section of the sea where they had initially emerged from the woods, and looking forward, they could now discern another shoreline across the water, heavily forested, much like the one they were currently investigating. However, this observation left them feeling let down.
